# Organizations

Organizations are the tenant boundary for the platform. They own organization-volume bindings, invitations, and org-scoped saved reading data.

## Mental Model

Think about organization access in two layers:

1. **Workspace access**: can this user enter the organization at all?
2. **Volume access**: which volumes can this user open?

Workspace access comes from Better Auth organization membership. Volume access comes from Better Auth team membership attached to the organization-volume binding. Admins and owners bypass the per-volume team check.

**Volume access is decoupled from seat billing.** A learner consumes one seat regardless of whether they have access to one volume or all of them. Admins control which volumes a learner can open at three explicit moments: invite time, post-acceptance edit, and new-volume creation. See `docs/billing/README.md` for the seat side of the model.

Orgs also carry a **lifecycle state** that gates learner onboarding ahead of billing checks. Self-service orgs are `active` from creation. Admin-provisioned orgs sit in `preview` until the customer completes activation Checkout — workspace access for owners and admins works normally, but learners are blocked centrally by the seat policy in `features/billing/policy.ts`. See `docs/provisioning.md`.

## Roles

| Role     | Capabilities                                                              |
| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `owner`  | Full control of org, settings, people, billing, and all volumes (bypass)  |
| `admin`  | Manage volumes, invites, and dashboards; access all volumes (bypass)      |
| `member` | Learner. Volume access is explicit — invite-time scope and post-invite editor decide |

Platform-level admins do not bypass org membership. Inside orgs they belong to, they can pass admin-only checks where the action middleware explicitly allows it.

## Organization-volume binding

Organizations do not store CMS content locally. Each org-visible volume is represented by one `organization_volumes` row.

That row stores:

- `id` — stable route key for org-scoped pages
- `organizationId` — owning org
- `teamId` — Better Auth team for access control
- `status` — generation or import lifecycle
- `label` — local fallback label, usually initialized from the Payload title
- `cmsDocumentId` — immutable Payload volume ID
- `generationJobId` — external generation job ID, when applicable
- `learnerSyncDecidedAt` — non-null once the post-finalization "who should have access?" prompt has been answered for generated volumes; pre-stamped for imports

This row is the join point between tenancy, generation, access control, dashboards, invitations, and saved reading analytics.

## Org surfaces

The organization UI is intentionally split into two surfaces.

### Shared org launchpad

`/org/[orgSlug]` is the shared launchpad for all org members.

- Shows the org-visible volume grid
- Filters non-admin members down to the bindings they have team access to
- Uses volume status to decide whether a card points at the generation page, the shared volume page, or the per-volume dashboard

### Admin workspace

Admin responsibilities live in sibling routes under the same org slug:

| Route                            | Purpose                                                  |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/dashboard`       | Admin overview and volume management                     |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/generate`        | Admin content-acquisition hub                            |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/generate/upload` | PDF upload and generation                                |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/generate/import` | Existing-content import                                  |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/people`          | Members, invitations, pending invitations                |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/settings`        | Org settings and destructive actions                     |

The `(admin)` route group enforces org membership first, then admin or owner role, with the same membership-first platform-admin behavior used by admin actions.

## Role-aware landing

The platform distinguishes between:

- the **default landing page** for an org
- the **literal org launchpad route**

The root redirect is role-aware:

- admins land on `/org/[orgSlug]/dashboard`
- members land on `/org/[orgSlug]`

But the launchpad route remains stable at `/org/[orgSlug]`. "Back to org" links and "view as learner" links should continue to point there rather than to a role-aware home helper.

Authenticated users with no org membership and no live invitation land on `/welcome` — a no-access landing that surfaces their current email and a sign-out shortcut. The path used to be `/org/new`, which misled the dominant population (learners waiting for an invite) into creating duplicate orgs. The `/welcome` page also re-checks for a live pending invitation and redirects into the invite flow if one exists, mirroring the root redirect's logic for users who deep-link in.

## Sidebar model

The portal sidebar is organization-aware and binding-aware.

- Every org member gets a `Volumes` item pointing to `/org/[orgSlug]`
- Admins also get `Dashboard`, `Generate`, `People`, and `Settings`
- The volume list beneath that is driven by organization-volume bindings, not direct CMS routes

Volume links are status-aware:

- pending or processing bindings go to `/generation/[generationJobId]`
- completed bindings go to the per-volume dashboard for admins
- completed bindings go to the shared volume page for members

## Invitations

Invitations are org-scoped and issued in bulk. An admin pastes a list of emails, picks a role, and (for learner invites) picks a volume scope. The server classifies each email against current org state before any invitations are written.

### Effective status

Better Auth stores invitation `status` as `pending | accepted | canceled | rejected` and never flips a stale `pending` to `expired`. The platform layers a derived **effective status** on top: `pending` past `expiresAt` becomes `expired`. The pure helper `effectiveInvitationStatus` (`src/features/organization/invitation-status.ts`) is the single source of truth for this projection. Use it everywhere — invitation tables, count badges, identity-aware screens, gate checks in actions like `resendInvitation`. Counting raw `status === 'pending'` rows would inflate badges with expired-but-stored-pending rows.

### Email normalization

All emails are lowercased and trimmed at the action boundary in `inviteWithVolumes`. Better Auth persists the lowercased form, so case-insensitive comparisons against `session.user.email` (at acceptance time) and across pending-row lookups are consistent. The case-insensitive `getFirstPendingInvitation` lookup uses the same convention.

### Bulk classification

Each email falls into exactly one of four buckets, computed server-side in one pass against current members and pending invitations:

- **create** — not a member, not pending. A new invitation row is written.
- **refresh** — already pending with the **same** requested role. Better Auth's `resend: true` path refreshes the existing row's expiry without creating a duplicate. No new seat is consumed.
- **alreadyMember** — already in the org in any role. Skipped with no write.
- **roleConflict** — already pending with a **different** role. Refused, not resent. Better Auth's resend path does not update the stored role, so silently resending would persist the old role under a success message. Admins resolve it by cancelling the pending invitation from the Pending Invitations tab and re-inviting.

Before classification, the action calls `cancelExpiredPendingInvitations` to mark expired-pending rows as canceled. Better Auth's own pending-row lookup filters expired, but leaving them as `status='pending'` confuses the admin UI and the seat classifier. Cleanup at the action boundary keeps the data layer honest.

The `cancelPendingInvitationsOnReInvite: true` Better Auth flag is enabled as defense in depth. It supersedes live duplicates only — the explicit cleanup above handles expired rows.

Any per-email `createInvitation` failure beyond these four buckets is collected as `failed` with its reason. The action returns all four buckets plus `failed` so the UI can render a single outcome panel rather than bail on the first error.

### Volume scope

Learner invitations carry a scope choice — admin's intent at invite time. Admin invitations ignore scope entirely (admins access all volumes via bypass).

| Scope            | Meaning                                                                          |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `all-current`    | Snapshot every org volume that exists at this moment. Future volumes are decided per-volume at the finalization prompt. |
| `selected`       | Explicit list of `organization_volume` IDs.                                      |

The action enforces snapshot semantics for `all-current`: it enumerates org volumes server-side at invite time and writes one `invitation_volumes` row per volume. The `accessScope` column on `invitations` records the original intent (`"all"` or `"selected"`) — Better Auth `additionalFields` extension, see `docs/better-auth-schema-runbook.md`.

`scope = "selected"` with an empty volume list is rejected at the action boundary. The acceptance hook would otherwise see zero scoped rows and fall back to the legacy "all current" path, silently overgranting access against the admin's stated intent.

The refresh path is delete-then-rewrite: a re-invite replaces the prior `invitation_volumes` rows so the new scope is the truth, not a union. The `accessScope` column is updated in the same write.

### Acceptance and team sync

`afterAcceptInvitation` branches on the invitation's `accessScope`:

- **non-null** (post-feature) — invitation_volumes is the source of truth. Learner is added to exactly the team_members rows that map to those volumes. An empty result is honored as-is — the admin's selection resolved to nothing.
- **null** (legacy / pre-feature) — fall back to the all-current sync (`syncLearnerToAllOrganizationVolumeTeams`). Required so in-flight links at deploy time don't accept into zero access.

The hook deletes `invitation_volumes` rows after sync. The `accessScope` column is left set on the invitation row.

### Seat enforcement

Seat enforcement is independent of volume scope. The four seat-policy entry points in `features/billing/policy.ts` each know their own budget question; the invitation flow uses `checkInviteCapacityForBatch({ orgId, batchSize })` against the **net-new** invites only. Refreshes and already-members are excluded from the batch because their seats are either reserved (pending) or consumed (member). A failure blocks the whole batch with a single message; no partial sends.

Pending invitations count toward the budget at every seat-policy entry point, including acceptance — see `docs/adr/0002-seat-budget-counts-pending-everywhere.md`. An invitation issued before a downgrade can therefore fail to accept after the downgrade if `members + pending > entitled`; the invitee sees a learner-facing rejection ("contact an organization admin") rather than admin-targeted copy.

Seat-consuming invitation writes are serialized by an org-level seat lock that wraps both the seat check and the Better Auth write. A database trigger on pending learner invitations is the final backstop. The acceptance path uses the same pattern.

Admin invitations bypass seat checks (admins are free during pilot).

### Identity-aware acceptance screen

`/org/invite/[invitationId]` is a thin server component that resolves the invitation against current state and renders one of nine branches. Each branch surfaces (a) what the platform expects, (b) what's true now, and (c) a specific recovery action.

| Branch          | Trigger                                                                       |
| --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `not-found`     | Invitation ID does not resolve                                                |
| `expired`       | `effectiveInvitationStatus(invitation) === "expired"`                         |
| `accepted`      | Already accepted                                                              |
| `canceled`      | Stored status is `canceled`                                                   |
| `rejected`      | Stored status is `rejected`                                                   |
| `signed-out`    | No active session                                                             |
| `wrong-account` | Session email does not match the invitation email (case-insensitive compare) |
| `already-member`| Session user is already a member of the inviting org                          |
| `ready`         | Otherwise — the live accept/decline UI                                        |

The `signed-out` branch links to `/login` with the invitation as the callback URL; the login page reads the invitation context and prefills the email input (read-only) plus annotates OAuth buttons with the expected email. The `wrong-account` branch offers a `SwitchAccountButton` that signs out and redirects back to the invite link.

The `acceptOrganizationInvitation` action does its own session-email check before invoking Better Auth's accept call — defense in depth against races where the page rendered as `ready` but the session changed before submit. Branch-specific `ActionError` messages distinguish expired / canceled / accepted / different-email failures so the UI can render meaningful errors.

Entry points: `src/app/(auth)/org/invite/[invitationId]/page.tsx`, `src/app/(auth)/org/invite/[invitationId]/_components/state-views.tsx`, `src/app/(auth)/org/invite/[invitationId]/actions.ts`.

### Invitation emails

The Better Auth `sendInvitationEmail` hook is intentionally a no-op. Better Auth fires the hook synchronously inside `auth.api.createInvitation`, before the action can write `invitation_volumes` rows — emailing from the hook would always show an empty (or stale, on resend) course list. Emails are sent from app code via `sendInvitationEmailForId` (`src/features/organization/invitation-emails.ts`), called by `inviteWithVolumes` after the rows are written.

The `resendInvitation` action follows the same pattern: validate effective status is `pending`, call `auth.api.createInvitation` with `resend: true`, assert the returned invitation ID matches the input (defense against a race window where Better Auth created a fresh row instead of refreshing), then send the email. The admin table calls this action instead of doing client-side cancel + invite, which previously dropped scope on every resend.

Entry points: `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/actions.ts` (`inviteWithVolumes`, `resendInvitation`, `updateMemberVolumeAccess`, `decideVolumeLearnerSync`), `src/features/billing/counts.ts` (partition queries), `src/features/organization/member-volume-access.ts` (pure validation + diff for the editor).

## People management

The People page has three tabs:

| Tab                  | Default | Contents                                                                  |
| -------------------- | ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Members              | yes     | Accepted org members with name, email, role, volume access summary, actions |
| Send Invitations     |         | Tag-input for emails, role radio cards, volume scope picker               |
| Pending Invitations  |         | Table with effective status; resend/cancel hidden for non-pending rows    |

The Members tab is the anchor for post-acceptance volume access management. Each learner row exposes:

- **Volume access** column: `All (3)`, `2 of 3`, or `No volumes yet`. Tooltip lists volume names. Admin/owner rows show `All (admin)` muted.
- **Manage access** action: opens a side-sheet with a `VolumeChecklist` showing the learner's current selection. Save calls `updateMemberVolumeAccess`.

`updateMemberVolumeAccess` is diff-based and idempotent. Pure validation and diff math live in `src/features/organization/member-volume-access.ts` and cover five invariants: target user is a member of this org, target role is `member` (admins/owners rejected), all volume IDs belong to this org, all target volumes have non-null `teamId` (otherwise typed `VOLUME_NEEDS_REPAIR` error), and the diff is scoped to this org's `teamIds` only — a request can never touch a team in another org.

Pending invitation counts in the People tab badge and the dashboard summary use `effectiveInvitationStatus` to filter expired-but-stored-pending rows out of the count.

## Settings

Admins and owners can:

- rename the organization
- change the org slug
- delete the organization

Deleting an organization is server-owned and billing-aware. For normal pilot subscriptions, deletion is blocked while Stripe still has a live subscription relationship; the owner must cancel billing and wait until the subscription ends before deleting the tenant. Internal-plan orgs are the exception: the delete action verifies the live subscription is on `STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`, cancels that $0 subscription immediately, and then deletes the org locally.

Deleting an organization removes Better Auth organization state, organization-volume bindings, invitations, billing rows, and org-scoped saved reading data by cascade. It does not delete the underlying Payload content.

## Volume access

Non-admin members need both:

- membership in the organization
- membership in the Better Auth team attached to the organization-volume binding

Team membership is created at three explicit moments:

1. **Invitation acceptance** — `afterAcceptInvitation` reads `invitation_volumes` and adds the learner to those teams (or all-current as legacy fallback).
2. **Post-acceptance edit** — admin uses **Manage access** in the Members tab; `updateMemberVolumeAccess` diffs and writes `team_members` directly.
3. **New volume finalization** — when a generated volume completes, the admin answers the finalization prompt (`Add for all current learners` / `Don't add anyone yet`) on the generation status page; `decideVolumeLearnerSync` syncs accordingly and stamps `learnerSyncDecidedAt`. Imports auto-sync at creation and stamp the column at insert time.

Admins and owners bypass team checks; their access is implicit. Editing team membership for an admin/owner target is rejected at the action.

## Org creation

There are two paths:

- **Self-service** (`/org/new`) goes through Better Auth's organization plugin. The creator becomes owner, the org starts in `lifecycleState='active'`, and the form lands on the new org's launchpad (`/org/[slug]`). The org layout calls `setActiveOrganization` on render so the sidebar's active marker tracks without an explicit setActive call.
- **Admin-provisioned** (`/admin/launches`) bypasses Better Auth's plugin for sales-led customers. The platform admin who submits the launch is not made a member of the resulting org; the customer's owner becomes owner through the normal invitation acceptance flow. The org starts in `lifecycleState='preview'` until the owner completes activation Checkout. See `docs/provisioning.md`.

Owner-role invitations exist only on the provisioning path — Better Auth's `createInvitation` rejects `role='owner'`, so the provisioning service inserts the invitation row directly inside its transaction. Acceptance, hooks, and resend semantics otherwise match plugin-created invitations.

## Related docs

- `docs/billing/README.md` — seat model, seat enforcement on invitations, billing/access decoupling
- `docs/volumes.md` — binding model, route-level gating, finalization prompt
- `docs/content-generation.md` — how bindings are created and finalized
- `docs/auth.md` — invitation acceptance hook, identity-aware screens, login invitation context
- `docs/better-auth-schema-runbook.md` — adding columns via `additionalFields` (used for `invitations.accessScope`)

# Volumes

In the current platform architecture, all documented org volumes are Payload-backed organization-volume bindings.

## Mental Model

There are two distinct “volume” concepts:

1. **Payload volume**: the CMS record that stores content and metadata.
2. **Organization volume**: the platform binding row that makes that content visible inside an organization.

The platform routes, dashboards, access control, invitations, and saved reading analytics all use the organization volume. Payload remains the source of truth for the content itself.

## Binding vs content

The organization-volume binding exists to answer platform questions that Payload should not own:

- which org owns this volume
- who can access it
- whether generation is pending, processing, completed, or failed
- what local label should be shown before Payload content is readable

The Payload volume exists to answer content questions:

- title
- chapters and lesson ordering
- lesson content and embedded activities
- summaries and descriptions

## Route model

Org-scoped routes use the binding ID, not a CMS slug:

| Route                                         | Meaning                     |
| --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `/org/[orgSlug]`                              | Shared org launchpad        |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/dashboard`                    | Org-level admin dashboard   |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/generation/[generationJobId]` | Generation polling page     |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]`                    | Shared org volume page          |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/dashboard`          | Per-volume cohort overview      |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/dashboard/[userId]` | Per-learner drill-in (admin)    |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/dashboard/integrations/scorm` | Admin SCORM console (download, learner roster, integrations). See `docs/scorm/`. |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/[pageId]`           | Lesson page                     |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/complete`           | Completion page                 |

Route resolution works as:

`volumeId -> organization_volumes row -> cmsDocumentId -> Payload fetch`

This keeps org routing stable even if the Payload slug or title changes.

## Access control

Access is enforced in layers:

1. Org layouts verify organization membership.
2. The shared launchpad filters visible bindings for non-admin members.
3. Shared volume and lesson layouts verify team membership for non-admins.
4. Admin routes gate admins and owners only.
5. `volumeActionClient` enforces the same model for mutations.

Admins and owners bypass team checks, but still need org membership. Team membership for learners is admin-controlled — at invite time via the scope picker, and post-acceptance via the People tab's Manage access editor. See `docs/organization.md` for the full model.

## Display names

The platform stores a local `label` on the organization-volume binding, but that is not the canonical title.

Use `label` for:

- generation status pages
- pending, processing, or failed bindings
- any display that needs a readable fallback before Payload content is available

Use the Payload title for steady-state displays when `cmsDocumentId` exists:

- shared launchpad cards
- org dashboard tables
- per-volume admin headers
- sidebar volume list
- lesson and learner-facing UI

The platform now resolves these steady-state titles through a batched Payload lookup rather than one CMS request per binding. A failed batched lookup falls back to `label`, so a Payload outage degrades naming without breaking the portal shell.

## Lifecycle

### Generated volume

1. PDF upload creates a binding with `generationJobId`, `status=pending`, `label=file name`, and `learnerSyncDecidedAt=null`. The Better Auth team is created at this point but no learners are added.
2. Polling advances the status through `processing`.
3. Once Payload can return the volume, the binding is finalized with `cmsDocumentId`, `status=completed`, and `label` initialized from the Payload title.
4. The generation status page renders the **finalization prompt** for the admin: *Add for all current learners* or *Don't add anyone yet*. The choice runs `decideVolumeLearnerSync` and stamps `learnerSyncDecidedAt`. The page holds its auto-redirect to the volume until the prompt is answered.

If the admin closes the page before answering, the volume sits accessible to admins/owners only. The People tab's Manage access editor is the recovery path — admins can grant access per-learner later.

### Imported volume

1. Admin chooses an existing Payload volume from the import UI (browse or by-slug).
2. The platform creates a binding immediately with `cmsDocumentId`, `status=completed`, `label` from the current Payload title, and `learnerSyncDecidedAt=NOW()`. Existing learners are synced to the new team in the same flow.

Imports skip the finalization prompt because they're a power-user action where the volume is accessible immediately and the admin's intent is presumed to be "give my org access to this." `createOrganizationVolume` infers the import path from a missing `generationJobId` and pre-stamps `learnerSyncDecidedAt` so the prompt UI never fires for them.

Both generated and imported volumes end up with the same binding shape; the only difference is whether `learnerSyncDecidedAt` was set by the admin's prompt answer or by the import path's auto-stamp.

## Reader data key

Org-scoped reading data is keyed by `organizationVolumeId`, not by slug or `cmsDocumentId`.

This affects:

- page progress
- page completion logs
- chunk progress
- lesson time
- summary, quiz, cloze, and CRI activity logs

This is the main reason the binding ID must stay stable and first-class.

## Deletion

Deleting a volume deletes the organization-volume binding, not the Payload content.

Because org-scoped reading data references the binding row by foreign key, deleting the binding can cascade:

- progress rows
- activity rows
- lesson-time rows

The Better Auth team for that volume is also removed.

## Current scope

The intended mental model is:

- org volumes are Payload-backed
- org routes are ID-based
- the platform does not use CMS slugs as org identifiers

Some compatibility code still exists from the earlier dual-provider period, but new development should assume Payload-backed org volumes.

A complementary non-tracking surface lives at `/explore/*` for marketing-style demos. Public volumes share the content rendering pipeline but bypass org tenancy entirely — no binding row, no team, no learner records. See `docs/public-volumes.md`.

## Related docs

- `docs/organization.md` — tenant and invitation model
- `docs/cms-data-system.md` — Payload content and metadata read patterns
- `docs/content-generation.md` — binding creation and finalization

# Authentication

**Key Files:**

- `src/lib/server/auth.ts` - Better Auth instance with plugins
- `src/lib/server/session.ts` - getCurrentUser() helper
- `src/lib/client/auth.ts` - Auth client with plugins
- `src/db/schemas/auth.schema.ts` - User, session, org, team, member, invitation tables
- `src/app/(auth)/` - Auth UI flows (login, invitations, 2FA, password reset)

Passwordless authentication via Better Auth (https://www.better-auth.com/) with magic link, OAuth, and optional two-factor authentication.

## How Login Works

1. User visits `/login`, enters email or clicks OAuth provider
2. Magic link: redirected to `/check-email` → clicks email link → authenticated
3. OAuth: redirected to provider → authenticated on callback
4. If 2FA enabled: redirected to `/two-factor` → enter TOTP or backup code
5. Redirects to `callbackUrl` search param if present, otherwise `/`

**Account linking**: Same email = same account across all methods.

**Sessions**: 30-day expiry, refresh daily on activity.

**Invitation context**: When `callbackUrl` points to an invitation (`/org/invite/[id]`), the login page reads the live invitation, shows the inviting org and expected email, and prefills the email input as read-only. OAuth buttons are annotated with the expected email. Already-signed-in users whose session email differs from the invitation email see a wrong-account variant with a `SwitchAccountButton` instead of the normal post-login redirect. Authenticated users with no org membership and no live invitation land on `/welcome` rather than the org-creation form, so the dominant population (learners waiting for an invite) doesn't get pushed into creating duplicate orgs.

## Routes

| Route             | Purpose                                         |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| `/login`          | Sign in with magic link or OAuth                |
| `/check-email`    | Post magic-link redirect                        |
| `/two-factor`     | 2FA verification during login                   |
| `/reset-password` | Set password (for 2FA enablement)               |
| `/welcome`        | No-access landing for authenticated users with no orgs and no live invite |
| `/settings`       | Session management, 2FA setup, account deletion |

## Two-Factor Authentication

TOTP-based 2FA using authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy, etc.).

**Prerequisite**: Users must have a password. Since login is passwordless, users first set a password via email reset flow.

**Setup flow**: No password → "Set Password" button sends reset email → User sets password → Enable 2FA via `authClient.twoFactor.enable()` → Scan QR code → Verify with 6-digit code via `authClient.twoFactor.verifyTotp()` → Save backup codes

**Login with 2FA**: Users choose between TOTP code or one-time backup code at `/two-factor`.

See `src/app/(centered)/settings/_components/security-section.tsx` for 2FA setup implementation.

## Account Deletion

Users can delete their account from `/settings`. Deletion is blocked if the user is the **sole owner** of any organization—must transfer ownership or delete the organization first.

**Flow**: Type email to confirm → server validates sole ownership → sends verification email via `authClient.deleteUser()` → click link to complete deletion.

See `src/app/(centered)/settings/_components/delete-account-section.tsx` for implementation.

## Getting Current User

```ts
import { getCurrentUser } from "@/lib/server/session";

const user = await getCurrentUser();
// user.email, user.twoFactorEnabled, etc.
```

## Organization Plugin

The organization plugin has teams enabled (`teams: { enabled: true }`) to support per-volume access control. Each org-scoped volume gets a corresponding Better Auth team. See `docs/volumes.md` for the full gating strategy.

**Key schema**: `organization_volumes.teamId` links volumes to teams. The `invitations` table also carries an `accessScope` column (Better Auth `additionalFields`) recording the admin's invite-time scope intent — `"all"` or `"selected"`. See `docs/better-auth-schema-runbook.md` for the workflow used to add it.

**`afterAcceptInvitation` hook** — For learner invitations, the hook branches on the row's `accessScope`:

- **non-null** (post-feature) — read `invitation_volumes`, add the learner only to the volume teams it lists. An empty result is honored as-is; the admin's selection resolved to nothing.
- **null** (legacy) — fall back to syncing all current org volume teams. Required so any in-flight invitation links surviving deploy don't accept into zero access.

Admins bypass team checks entirely, so admin invitations skip team assignment. The hook always deletes `invitation_volumes` rows after sync.

`sendInvitationEmail` is intentionally a no-op. Better Auth fires it synchronously inside `auth.api.createInvitation`, before the action can write `invitation_volumes` — emailing from the hook would always show an empty (or stale, on resend) course list. Emails are sent from `inviteWithVolumes` and `resendInvitation` after the rows are written, via `sendInvitationEmailForId` (`src/features/organization/invitation-emails.ts`).

The app-owned invitation acceptance UI routes through a server action that holds the shared org seat lock around the full `auth.api.acceptInvitation()` call. The hook is still defense in depth and team-sync glue, but not the only race protection. Database triggers on `members` and pending learner `invitations` are the final backstop for raw Better Auth writes.

The `cancelPendingInvitationsOnReInvite: true` flag is enabled to supersede live duplicate pending rows — see `docs/organization.md` for the full classifier.

## Identity-Aware Invitation Acceptance

`/org/invite/[invitationId]` resolves the invitation against current state and renders one of nine branches (NotFound, Expired, Accepted, Canceled, Rejected, SignedOut, WrongAccount, AlreadyMember, Ready). Each branch surfaces the recipient's identity context and a specific recovery action — the `wrong-account` branch shows both the invited email and the current session email and offers a `SwitchAccountButton`; the `signed-out` branch links to `/login` with the invitation as `callbackUrl`. The `acceptOrganizationInvitation` action does its own session-email check before invoking Better Auth as defense against the page-state-stale race. See `docs/organization.md` for the full branch table and the identity-aware flow.

## OAuth 2.1 Provider

iTELL acts as an OAuth 2.1 / OIDC provider so the Payload CMS can authenticate users and retrieve volume editing permissions. See `docs/oauth-provider.md` for the full implementation guide (architecture, redirect flow, gotchas). See `docs/plans/oauth-provider-integration.md` for the Payload-facing consumer documentation.

## SCORM Bootstrap Plugin

A Better Auth plugin endpoint at `/api/auth/scorm/bootstrap` mints sessions for learners launching an iTELL volume from inside a customer LMS iframe. The plugin uses the same primitives Better Auth's own `/sign-in/*` routes use — `internalAdapter.createSession` followed by `setSessionCookie` — but applies `{sameSite: "none", secure: true, partitioned: true}` as a **per-call** cookie-attribute override so the resulting session cookie can survive the CHIPS cross-site iframe partition.

First-party auth is entirely unchanged. The plugin's only additions to the `betterAuth({…})` config are the plugin registration itself and a `scormMode: boolean` field under `session.additionalFields` that marks sessions created through this path. First-party sessions leave `scormMode` at its default of `false`. The plugin body is still gated behind `SCORM_IFRAME_ENABLED`, and first-time learner creation is separately gated behind `SCORM_AUTO_PROVISION_ENABLED`.

The plugin now supports two outcomes after integration validation:

- reuse an existing `scorm_identities` row and mint a session for that learner
- auto-provision a new learner on first launch, subject to the org's normal seat limits, then mint the session

The SCORM identity row remains the canonical org-scoped record for LMS learner provenance. See `docs/scorm/README.md` for the full trust model, known security limitations, and rollout posture; see `docs/scorm/bootstrap.md` for the end-to-end launch flow.

# Server Actions

Server actions are the typed boundary for privileged writes and selected admin-only reads. They combine input validation with progressively stronger auth context.

## Mental Model

Each action client adds one layer of trust:

```
actionClient
  → authActionClient
      → orgActionClient → orgAdminActionClient / orgOwnerActionClient / volumeActionClient
      → platformAdminActionClient   (parallel branch — not org-scoped)
```

Business code should rely on that context instead of re-implementing membership checks inside each action.

## Action clients

### `authActionClient`

Use when the action needs only an authenticated user.

Provides:

- `ctx.user`

### `orgActionClient`

Use when the action is scoped to an organization.

Requires:

- `organizationId` in the input schema

Provides:

- `ctx.user`
- `ctx.org`
- `ctx.member`

Critical rule: org membership is enforced here first. If the caller is not a member of the organization, downstream action clients never run.

### `orgAdminActionClient`

Use when the action is limited to org admins or owners, or when an admin-only read should reuse the same policy.

Adds:

- admin or owner role enforcement
- membership-first platform-admin bypass
- `ctx.isAdmin`

The important nuance is ordering:

1. `orgActionClient` proves the caller belongs to the org
2. `orgAdminActionClient` proves they are an admin, owner, or eligible platform admin

This keeps admin UI gating and admin action gating aligned.

### `orgOwnerActionClient`

Use when the action is restricted to the org owner only. Currently used for all billing mutations.

Adds:

- owner role enforcement (no admin or platform-admin bypass)
- `ctx.isOwner`

This is intentionally stricter than `orgAdminActionClient` — billing operations are owner-only during pilot.

### `volumeActionClient`

Use when the action is scoped to a specific organization volume. Operates on the organization-volume **binding ID**, which is the key shared by access control, progress and activity tables, dashboard queries, and generation / deletion flows — not a CMS slug.

Requires:

- `organizationId`
- `volumeId`

Behavior, in order:

1. `orgActionClient` proves the caller belongs to the organization.
2. **Binding ownership check (unconditional).** The middleware looks up the `organizationVolumes` row under `ctx.org.id`. A missing row rejects with `"Volume not found in this organization"` — this covers both "volume does not exist" and "volume belongs to a different org." This check runs for every caller, including admins and owners, so a privileged actor cannot pass a `volumeId` from another tenant.
3. **Team membership check (non-admin org members only).** Org admins and org owners bypass the team check. Everyone else, including platform admins whose org role is only `member`, must be a member of the binding's Better Auth team.

Provides:

- the same org context as `orgActionClient`
- `ctx.volumeId` (trusted — derived from the resolved binding, not the raw client input)

The binding and team-membership resolution lives in `src/lib/server/volume-access-policy.ts`. `volumeActionClient` uses that shared policy, and route handlers that cannot use next-safe-action — currently the lesson-time beacon endpoint — must reuse it directly so emergency writes enforce the same tenant and team boundary.

Activity, completion, skip-credit, chunk-progress, and lesson-time writes all use `volumeActionClient`. Org admin and owner submissions are normal rows when they read outside preview mode; cohort dashboards exclude them at read time through the volume-cohort filter in `features/organization/learner-roles.ts`. Preview mode is the opt-out path for admins who want to inspect content without client-initiated progress writes.

### `platformAdminActionClient`

Use when the action is a platform-level operator tool that is **not** scoped to a single org. Used by `/admin/organizations` (attach / detach the internal plan), `/admin/public-volumes` (catalog mutations for the marketing surface), and `/admin/launches` (customer launch provisioning and deprovisioning). All three surfaces operate over platform-wide state with no org context.

Extends `authActionClient` directly, not `orgActionClient`, because it operates across the full org list rather than within a single tenant.

Adds:

- `ctx.user.role === "admin"` check (Better Auth admin plugin role)
- `ctx.isPlatformAdmin`

This is strictly distinct from the platform-admin bypass inside `orgAdminActionClient`. That bypass lets a platform admin pass org-admin checks _while still being an org member_. `platformAdminActionClient` does not require or imply org membership at all — it's the right fit for ops surfaces that operate over arbitrary org IDs (`/admin/organizations`, `/admin/launches`) or over platform-wide state with no org context (`/admin/public-volumes`). See `docs/billing/internal-plan.md`, `docs/public-volumes.md`, and `docs/provisioning.md` for the per-surface details.

This platform-admin behavior is intentionally different from `volumeActionClient`: platform admins can pass org-admin checks inside orgs they belong to, but they do not bypass volume team membership when their org role is only `member`.

## Read actions vs route reads

Most page reads are still plain server-component queries. But admin-only client workflows can use server actions for reads when that keeps the UI simpler.

Current examples:

- the import table uses `orgAdminActionClient` read actions behind React Query so pagination and search can stay client-driven without exposing a separate route handler
- the per-volume dashboard's submissions Sheet (`fetchLessonSubmissions` in `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/dashboard/actions.ts`) uses the same pattern to lazily fetch raw learner submissions only when the Sheet opens, rather than block the lesson timeline's Suspense boundary on data the user might never view

Use this pattern when:

- the read is permission-sensitive
- the caller is client-side
- the action middleware already expresses the right policy

The dashboard variant adds a second auth check inside the handler — the target `userId` must itself appear in the volume's learner set — because admin role alone is too broad: an admin shouldn't be able to fish for the activity of another admin or a user in a different volume by guessing IDs. See `docs/dashboard/drill-in.md`.

## Routes intentionally outside the chain

The `/api/public/*` route handlers (CRI scoring, summary grading, page-view ingest) sit outside the action-client chain by design: public volumes have no org or team to gate on. The substituted boundary is the `public_volumes` catalog plus per-route rate limits keyed on session and IP. Normal visitors resolve only published catalog rows; platform admins may preview draft or archived rows through a Better Auth platform-role check. The non-tracking invariant means none of these routes write learner records — page-view ingest is the only server-side write, and platform-admin preview skips that write. See `docs/public-volumes.md`.

## Design rules

- **Co-locate schemas.** Action input schemas live beside the action file and extend the shared org or volume base schemas.
- **Throw `ActionError` for business errors.** These are the only strings exposed to the client. Unexpected failures stay opaque.
- **Route vs feature split.** Route-level `actions.ts` owns orchestration, redirects, and revalidation. Feature modules own reusable query and persistence logic. Shared action middleware lives in `src/lib/action-client.ts`; reusable org-volume access policy lives in `src/lib/server/volume-access-policy.ts`.
- **Use the role helpers from `src/lib/roles.ts`.** `isOrgAdminRole` and `isPlatformAdminRole` keep platform-role and org-role domains separate and prevent typos. Do not compare role strings directly in handlers or middleware.
- **Polling flows can stay simple.** Read-style actions driven by client polling can be plain server actions when that keeps the callsite simpler. Generation status polling is the main example.

## Related docs

- `docs/organization.md` — org roles and invitations
- `docs/billing/README.md` — `orgOwnerActionClient` usage and seat enforcement
- `docs/billing/internal-plan.md` — `platformAdminActionClient` usage and the `/admin/organizations` attach/detach flow
- `docs/public-volumes.md` — `platformAdminActionClient` usage and the `/admin/public-volumes` catalog flow
- `docs/provisioning.md` — `platformAdminActionClient` usage and the `/admin/launches` provisioning flow
- `docs/volumes.md` — volume-scoped access model
- `docs/content-generation.md` — generation polling and import actions

# CMS Data System

Payload is the source of truth for content. The platform keeps only the minimum state needed to bind that content into organizations and track learner behavior.

## Mental Model

The CMS and the platform have different jobs:

- **Payload** owns content and content metadata.
- **The platform** owns tenancy, access control, generation workflow, binding labels, and learner analytics.

The critical boundary is the organization-volume binding:

`organization_volumes.id -> cmsDocumentId -> Payload volume`

That boundary lets the platform stay stable even if Payload titles or slugs change.

## Read patterns

Not every Payload read is the same. The platform has five distinct read patterns, each with its own cost/resilience tradeoff.

### 1. Content reads

Use these when the app needs the actual volume structure or lesson content.

Examples:

- shared org volume page
- lesson layout and page rendering
- generation finalization checks

These reads resolve the organization-volume binding first, then fetch full Payload content by `cmsDocumentId` or `pageId`. They parse the full Payload page shape (blocks, quiz, volume, parent, etc.), so they are the most exposed to schema drift — tolerance at the block level is what keeps them non-fatal.

### 2. Page metadata reads

Use this narrow path only for Next.js `generateMetadata`. It fetches `{title, slug, pageSummary}` via Payload's `select` projection and parses against a deliberately tiny Zod schema that never touches `contentBlocks`, `quiz`, `volume`, or any other polymorphic field.

Why it's separate from content reads: `generateMetadata` runs on every route render, and a thrown error inside it fails the whole Next.js route — not just the metadata. Routing metadata through a full page parse meant any schema drift anywhere in the page response would crash the lesson route. The narrow path is immune to that class of failure by construction, and the caller wraps it in try/catch with a `"Content unavailable"` fallback, so the metadata path is now non-throwing end to end.

Entry point: `getPayloadPageMeta(pageId)` in `src/features/cms/payload/queries.ts`. Schema: `PayloadPageMetaSchema`.

### 3. Page ownership reads

Lesson routes sometimes receive a page ID that is no longer present in the current volume manifest. That can mean either "this page belongs elsewhere" or "the CMS manifest changed and this learner has a stale URL." The platform resolves that ambiguity with a narrow page lookup that selects only the page's owning volume relation. If the page still belongs to the same Payload volume, the lesson route redirects to the learner's effective current page instead of showing a 404. If it belongs elsewhere or cannot be found, the route remains a real 404.

This read is intentionally separate from full content reads: it runs only on the missing-page branch and does not parse `contentBlocks`, quiz data, or rendered markdown.

Entry point: `getPayloadPageVolumeId(pageId)` in `src/features/cms/payload/queries.ts`. Schema: `PayloadPageVolumeIdSchema`.

A close relative — `getPayloadPagePublicSummary(pageId)` — returns `{cmsDocumentId, slug, chunkIds}` in one Payload roundtrip. Composed by the `validatePublicPageScope` step in `features/public-volumes/request-validation.ts` (used by the unauthenticated `/api/public/*` scoring routes) to validate that a client-supplied `(cmsDocumentId, pageId, chunkId)` triple actually belongs together before forwarding the request to the iTELL scoring API. The catalog gate alone would let an attacker pair a public `cmsDocumentId` with private page identifiers; this read closes that gap. See `docs/public-volumes.md`.

### 4. Batched title reads

Admin and portal shells often need many titles for already-bound volumes at once. The platform resolves those through one batched Payload `find` by ID rather than one request per binding.

Used by:

- sidebar volume list
- shared org launchpad
- org-level admin dashboard
- per-volume dashboard headers

If the batched CMS call fails, the UI falls back to the binding `label` so the portal shell still renders.

### 5. Catalog reads

Use these when the app is browsing content that is **not yet** bound into the org — currently just `/org/[orgSlug]/generate/import`.

The import table uses a dedicated paginated Payload list query: `depth: 0`, list metadata only, server-side search by title or slug, server-side pagination. Intentionally a different primitive from the full-content queries used by learner pages, so large catalogs don't accidentally trigger N+1 volume-with-chapters fetches.

## Tolerant per-block parsing

Payload models page content as a polymorphic `contentBlocks` array — editors can add any block type registered in the CMS schema. If the adapter treats the array as a strict schema, a single unknown or malformed block fails the whole page parse and cascades into a 500.

The adapter instead:

1. Parses `PayloadPageSchema.contentBlocks` as `z.array(z.unknown())` — the container shape only
2. Applies the strict discriminated union (`PayloadChunkBlockSchema | PayloadVideoBlockSchema`) per-block via `safeParse` inside the transform
3. Drops failing blocks with structured `ContentBlockWarning` records carrying `blockIndex`, `blockType`, `blockId`, and a `reason` discriminator (`unknown_type` / `parse_error` / `invalid_video_url`)
4. Threads the warnings through `LessonWithChunks.warnings` so admins in preview mode can see which blocks were dropped and why

This turns "one bad block fails the page" into "one bad block is dropped and surfaced to the editor who can fix it". Adding a new block type is a three-file change (schema, transform, render dispatcher) and never a breaking change to existing pages.

Entry point: `transformContentBlocks(rawBlocks)` in `src/features/cms/payload/transforms.ts`. Warning type: `ContentBlockWarning`. See `docs/content-rendering.md` for how the render dispatcher handles the resulting tagged union, and `docs/admin-tools.md` for how warnings surface to admins.

## Volume content reads and the versions quirk

For org-scoped volume pages, the adapter resolves `volumeId → cmsDocumentId`, fetches the volume by ID, and renders the current Payload title, chapters, and lesson ordering. The platform does not mirror chapter structure or lesson content into relational tables — every request hits Payload.

The one subtlety: the adapter fetches volumes through `GET /volumes/versions` rather than `GET /volumes/{id}?draft=true`. Two Payload quirks forced this:

- `GET /volumes/{id}?draft=true` can still return the latest _published_ snapshot instead of the latest draft, so newly added items live only in the admin UI until the next publish
- On that snapshot, polymorphic `items` sometimes come back as bare numeric IDs instead of populated objects, which the transform would otherwise drop

The adapter reads the latest version through the versions endpoint, fetches chapters separately, runs a defensive backfill pass for any page items that came back as bare IDs, and transforms the populated shape into platform domain types. If that path produces no lessons, it falls back to a direct `pages` lookup by `volume` and treats volume-parent pages as standalone lessons. That fallback covers imported Payload volumes whose pages are linked to the volume but whose latest volume version has an empty `items` array. Net effect: the platform sees the same latest draft-or-published structure that Payload's admin UI sees, including flat volumes.

Lesson page content is fetched per-page by `pageId`, not through the volume tree. Chunk rendering and CRI resolution happen after the Payload read, not inside the binding layer.

## What the platform stores

The platform does **not** mirror lesson or chunk content, chapter structure as first-class relational data, or CMS titles/slugs as canonical org identifiers.

It stores:

- organization-volume bindings
- learner progress and completion logs
- activity submissions
- lesson-time tracking
- invitation-to-volume mappings

## Why ID-based reads

Using `cmsDocumentId` instead of slug solves two problems:

1. **Rename safety**: Payload slug changes do not invalidate platform bindings.
2. **Single source of truth**: the platform stops pretending it owns mutable content metadata.

This is also why org routes use `volumeId` (the binding id) and not the CMS slug.

## Provider state

The codebase still contains legacy dual-provider residue from the earlier Strapi migration period. The intended mental model is:

- org-scoped volumes are Payload-backed
- org routes resolve through `cmsDocumentId`
- new development should assume Payload for org content

Treat remaining dual-provider types as compatibility residue, not as the current architecture.

## Related docs

- `docs/volumes.md` — binding model and routes
- `docs/content-generation.md` — how bindings are created and finalized
- `docs/content-rendering.md` — how content chunks become React output, including the video render path
- `docs/admin-tools.md` — how dropped-block warnings surface in preview mode

# Billing

Stripe-based seat billing at the organization level. Each org maps to one Stripe Customer and at most one active subscription. Seats represent learner slots, not named assignments.

## Mental Model

Billing sits next to the organization model, not on top of the access-control layer. It answers three questions:

1. **How many learners can this org have?** The mirrored Stripe subscription quantity.
2. **Who counts as a learner?** Any org member with `role = "member"`. Owners and admins are free during pilot.
3. **What does a seat cost?** A flat per-seat charge, regardless of how many volumes that learner can open.

A seat is a billing unit, not an authorization grant. Volume access is decided independently by `team_members` rows — admins set scope at invite time and edit it post-acceptance. See `docs/organization.md` for the access side. The seat math reads `members.role = "member"` (plus pending member-role invitations); it never inspects team membership.

Stripe is authoritative for money. The local `organization_billing` row is the hot-path access cache. Invitation checks, SCORM auto-provisioning, generation quota checks, and volume access must not call Stripe synchronously.

Internal test orgs can bypass seat and generation-quota enforcement through a dedicated $0 Stripe product with Entitlements. That platform-admin-only path lives in `docs/billing/internal-plan.md`.

Orgs in `lifecycleState='preview'` short-circuit the seat policy ahead of every other check — no Stripe customer, no subscription, no learner onboarding. Activation flips lifecycle to `active` and the standard billing rules below take over. See `docs/provisioning.md` for the full provisioning flow.

## Business Decisions

### Organization as billing unit

One organization equals one Stripe Customer. One organization has at most one active Stripe subscription. Volume count is irrelevant to billing: an org with 1 volume and an org with 10 volumes pays the same amount for the same number of seats.

### Seat model

- A seat is consumed at org membership, not at content access. The same seat is charged whether the learner can open one volume or every volume.
- Pending invitations for `role = "member"` reserve seats.
- Owners and admins are free during pilot.
- Removing a learner frees a seat slot, but does not reduce the subscription quantity.
- Billing action inputs are capped at 1,000 seats for pilot safety.

### Pricing and trial

Pilot pricing is $6 USD per seat per month, billed monthly and open-ended.

- **Seat increases** are immediate. Active subscriptions invoice prorated charges immediately; trialing subscriptions update quantity without charge.
- **Seat decreases** are scheduled for the next billing cycle through a Stripe subscription schedule. No mid-cycle refunds.
- **Free trial** is one month for orgs that request fewer than 20 seats at signup. Card collection happens up front. Trial usage is durable: once `trialUsedAt` is set, the org cannot get another trial even if the subscription is later canceled.

Trial duration is per-org via `organizations.trialPolicy` (`none` for sales-led launches, `standard_30d` for self-service and the historical default). The pure mapper `trialParamsForCheckout` translates the policy plus local trial eligibility into Stripe's `trial_period_days` parameter at Checkout creation.

### Billing permissions

Only the org owner can manage billing: subscribe, change seats, open the Stripe Portal, cancel, and resume. Admins and members have no billing mutation access. `orgOwnerActionClient` enforces this boundary.

### Payment failure and tax

During pilot, payment failures do not lock learners out. Stripe dunning runs, the billing UI surfaces `past_due` / `requires_action`, and Slack/ops notifications make the state visible.

Tax is not implemented in the app. A CEO/accounting sign-off is required before live charges begin: decide whether Stripe Tax is enabled, which tax code applies, what billing address or tax ID data is collected, and which jurisdictions matter.

## Seat Enforcement

Seat enforcement is layered. The user-facing checks provide clear errors; the database trigger is the race-tight backstop.

### Application policy

`features/billing/policy.ts` exposes four intent-specific entry points, each named for the seat-consuming path it gates. They share one internal `computeSeatBudget` snapshot (lifecycle, billing row, member count, pending invitation count) so callers do not re-litigate "what counts toward the budget" at every callsite:

| Entry point                     | Used by                                                   | Budget rule                                          |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| `checkInviteCapacityForBatch`   | Bulk learner invitation creation (admin invite UI)        | `members + pending + batchSize ≤ entitled`           |
| `checkInviteAcceptance`         | Invitation acceptance (user-side action + auth hook)      | `members + pending ≤ entitled`                       |
| `checkScormProvisionCapacity`   | SCORM first-launch learner provisioning                   | `members + pending + 1 ≤ entitled`                   |
| `checkRolePromotionToMember`    | Role change into `member` (admin role-change UI + hook)   | `members + pending + 1 ≤ entitled`                   |

Pending learner invitations count toward the budget at **every** entry point — see `docs/adr/0002-seat-budget-counts-pending-everywhere.md`. The behavior consequence: a pending invitation that pushes the org over its entitlement can block a SCORM provision, an admin → member role promotion, or an invite acceptance after a subscription downgrade.

The result type carries enough information for minimally specific user-facing messages without bespoke copy: `{ allowed: false, reason: 'no_seats', entitled, currentMembers, currentPending, requestedDelta }`. `features/billing/messages.ts` turns those into copy for the three audiences (admin invite, admin role-change, invitee acceptance).

Bulk invitations count only net-new learner invites. Refreshing a same-role pending invite and skipping an existing member do not consume another seat.

### Advisory locks

`withOrgSeatLock` serializes the app-owned seat-consuming write paths by organization. The lock wraps the check plus the write, not just the check:

```
seat-consuming action
  → withOrgSeatLock(orgId)
    → checkInviteCapacityForBatch / checkInviteAcceptance /
      checkScormProvisionCapacity / checkRolePromotionToMember
    → Better Auth / membership / invitation write
```

Seat increases also take the seat lock until the returned Stripe subscription has been synced into the mirror, so a concurrent invite or SCORM launch does not observe the old seat count.

### Database trigger

The `members` and pending `invitations` tables have trigger-level enforcement for learner seats. The trigger locks the org's billing row, checks `unlimited-seats`, requires a subscription for normal orgs, and rejects inserts or role/status changes that would exceed `seatCount`.

This protects raw Better Auth paths and other future writes that bypass the server actions. The trigger asymmetry is deliberate: the invitation trigger counts members + pending; the member trigger counts members only. Neither one alone enforces the full reserved-seat model — that is the app policy's job above. The trigger is intentionally not the user experience layer; application checks should still run first so users see domain-specific messages.

Entry points: `src/features/billing/policy.ts`, `src/features/billing/locks.ts`, `drizzle-migrations/0010_seat-enforcement-triggers.sql`.

### Anti-pattern: inviting learners as admins

Because admin and owner invites bypass the seat policy and the matching DB triggers, it's tempting to invite real students with `role='admin'` when an org has no active subscription — most often as a workaround in a preview org, or in a self-service org that hasn't paid yet. **Don't.** It works but breaks four things:

1. **Privilege scope.** Admins can rename the org, invite and remove members, edit team and volume access, and change roles. A class of 30 students with admin privileges has the keys to the customer's org.
2. **Dashboards.** The volume-cohort filter in `features/organization/learner-roles.ts` excludes `role IN ('admin','owner')` from aggregates at read time. Learner activity, completion, time-on-task, and streak data for those students will be invisible to the customer's own reports.
3. **Volume scoping.** Admins implicitly access *all* org volumes via `accessScope='all'`. They cannot be restricted to a specific team or volume.
4. **Conversion at pay time.** Demoting `admin → member` fires the seat gate per row; once the converted count exceeds the paid `seatCount`, further conversions fail with `no_seats`. There is no bulk demote affordance.

The supported paths for "real students access without a real subscription" are the trial (`trialPolicy='standard_30d'` at launch — see `docs/provisioning.md`) and the internal-plan entitlement (see `docs/billing/internal-plan.md`). Both keep the seat math, dashboards, and volume scope honest.

## Lifecycle Rules

### Checkout

Checkout uses hosted Stripe Checkout with `payment_method_types: ["card"]`. Before creating a session, the app ensures/reuses the org customer and checks live Stripe for an existing live subscription. The Checkout request uses a short bucketed idempotency key for retry safety.

There are two entry actions sharing a common builder:

- `createCheckoutSession` — self-service. Refuses orgs in `lifecycleState='preview'` so the wrong subscription metadata can't be painted on a preview org.
- `activatePreviewBilling` — preview-only. Reads the org's `trialPolicy` and `provisionedFromLaunchId`, stamps `provisionedVia='admin_launch'` and the launch ID onto `subscription_data.metadata` so the webhook can flip `preview → active` on completion.

The webhook path is the duplicate-subscription defense. Any inbound subscription event for the same org customer that mismatches a live mirror — regardless of which event type carried it (subscription.created, checkout.session.completed, or any invoice.*) — is treated as a duplicate. The handler preserves the original mirrored subscription, cancels the duplicate, refunds when the charge can be identified confidently, and alerts Slack when manual refund review is needed.

### Cancellation and resume

Self-service cancellation sets `cancel_at_period_end = true`. Access continues through the period end or trial end, the billing page shows the pending cancellation date, and the owner can resume before the subscription ends.

If a seat reduction is staged (a `subscription_schedule` is attached), the cancel, resume, and add-seats flows release the schedule first and clear `pendingSeatCount`. Stripe blocks direct subscription mutations while a schedule is active, so the staged reduction must be dropped to perform the new action.

When `customer.subscription.deleted` arrives, the mirror is cleared through the cancellation snapshot writer. That writer clears subscription fields and entitlements atomically so an org cannot retain internal bypasses after cancellation.

### Organization deletion

Org deletion is billing-aware and server-owned. It is blocked while the org has a live pilot subscription relationship, including active, trialing, past-due, incomplete, unpaid, or pending-cancellation states. The owner must cancel billing first and wait until access ends.

Internal-plan orgs are the exception: if live Stripe confirms the subscription's first item price matches `STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`, deletion cancels that $0 subscription immediately and then deletes the org locally. The later Stripe cancellation webhook can safely no-op because the org row has already cascaded away.

Preview-state orgs follow a separate path. Deprovisioning is initiated from `/admin/launches/[id]`, requires slug confirmation, and refuses when `organization_billing` is non-terminal — this guards the race window between `checkout.session.completed` and the lifecycle flip. See `docs/provisioning.md`.

Entry points: `src/features/billing/org-deletion.ts`, `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/settings/actions.ts`.

## Billing State

The `organization_billing` table mirrors key Stripe state for fast local lookups:

| Column                      | Purpose                                            |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `stripeCustomerId`          | Stripe Customer for this org                       |
| `stripeSubscriptionId`      | Active subscription identity, null when none       |
| `stripePriceId`             | Mirrored price ID used for plan classification     |
| `seatCount`                 | Currently enforced learner seat cap                |
| `pendingSeatCount`          | Scheduled reduction for next cycle                 |
| `stripeStatus`              | Raw Stripe subscription status                     |
| `billingState`              | App-level enum for stable UI semantics             |
| `trialEndsAt`               | Current trial end, when any                        |
| `trialUsedAt`               | Durable free-trial marker                          |
| `trialSourceSubscriptionId` | Subscription that consumed the trial               |
| `currentPeriodEnd`          | Next billing boundary                              |
| `cancelAtPeriodEnd`         | Whether cancellation is pending                    |
| `entitlements`              | Mirrored Stripe Entitlements for internal bypasses |

`stripeStatus` preserves Stripe's raw truth. `billingState` maps Stripe states to app semantics (`none`, `trialing`, `active`, `past_due`, `requires_action`, `canceled`). `invoice.payment_action_required` can temporarily override the subscription-level mapping to `requires_action`.

Entitlements are webhook-owned. The only writers for `entitlements` are `writeEntitlementSnapshot` and `writeCancellationSnapshot`; subscription sync intentionally cannot touch the column.

## Related Docs

- `docs/billing/stripe-integration.md` — Stripe SDK, checkout, webhooks, reconciliation, Slack observability
- `docs/billing/internal-plan.md` — platform-admin bypass for internal test orgs
- `docs/provisioning.md` — admin-driven preview-lifecycle org creation and activation
- `docs/organization.md` — tenant model, invitations, roles
- `docs/action.md` — action middleware including `orgOwnerActionClient`
- `docs/auth.md` — Better Auth hooks and invitation acceptance
