# Stripe Integration

Technical details for the Stripe billing integration. For business rules and the billing mental model, start with `docs/billing/README.md`.

## Source-of-Truth Rule

Stripe is authoritative for money. The iTELL mirror is the hot-path access cache.

- Stripe owns subscription status, payment status, invoice state, quantity, price, current period, cancellation state, and disputes.
- `organization_billing` exists so request-time access checks can stay local and fast.
- App-initiated mutations verify live Stripe state before changing money state.
- Successful app mutations sync the returned Stripe object into the mirror for immediate billing-page UX.
- Webhooks are the durable async correction path.
- Daily reconciliation bounds drift between Stripe and the mirror.

The exception is `entitlements`: subscription mutation responses do not include active entitlement summaries, so entitlement state is written only by the entitlement-summary webhook and the cancellation snapshot writer.

## SDK Setup

The Stripe Node SDK is initialized once at `src/lib/server/stripe.ts` with `import "server-only"` to prevent accidental client-side import. The SDK API version is explicitly pinned to `2026-03-25.dahlia`.

The Dashboard webhook endpoint has its own API version and must be pinned to the same version. The app can preflight this through `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_ENDPOINT_ID`; upgrading the SDK and webhook endpoint version should be one explicit piece of work, not accidental dependency churn.

No client-side Stripe.js is used. Checkout and portal flows use Stripe-hosted pages.

## Stripe Object Mapping

| Stripe Object                       | iTELL Equivalent                       | Relationship                                                                               |
| ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Customer                            | Organization                           | 1:1. Created or reused when the owner starts billing or an internal plan is attached.      |
| Product                             | Pilot plan and Internal plan           | Pilot is the paying tier. Internal is a $0 entitlement carrier for platform-admin testing. |
| Price                               | Pilot seat price and Internal $0 price | IDs stored as `STRIPE_PILOT_PRICE_ID` and `STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`.                      |
| Subscription                        | Org billing relationship               | At most one live subscription per org customer.                                            |
| Subscription Item                   | Seat line item                         | `quantity = seatCount`.                                                                    |
| Checkout Session                    | Initial purchase                       | Hosted redirect for card collection and subscription creation.                             |
| Billing Portal Session              | Payment and receipt management         | Hosted redirect for payment method updates and invoice history only.                       |
| Invoice / Charge / Refund / Dispute | Revenue events                         | Mirrored indirectly through subscription sync and Slack/reconciliation signals.            |

## Environment Variables

| Variable                                 | Purpose                                                                                                                                       |
| ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`                      | Server-side Stripe key. Restricted keys are preferred in production; both `sk_live_` and `rk_live_` are treated as live mode by cron routing. |
| `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`                  | Webhook signature verification secret.                                                                                                        |
| `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_ENDPOINT_ID`             | Dashboard webhook endpoint ID for API-version preflight.                                                                                      |
| `STRIPE_PILOT_PRICE_ID`                  | Price ID for the $6/seat/month pilot product.                                                                                                 |
| `STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`               | Price ID for the $0/month internal plan. Required for internal-plan attach/delete flows.                                                      |
| `STRIPE_BILLING_PORTAL_CONFIGURATION_ID` | Pinned portal configuration ID.                                                                                                               |
| `CRON_SECRET`                            | Bearer token required by `/api/cron/billing-reconciliation`.                                                                                  |
| `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN`                        | Bot token for billing notifications. Optional; missing token makes Slack posts no-op.                                                         |
| `SLACK_CHANNEL_REVENUE`                  | Live revenue channel ID.                                                                                                                      |
| `SLACK_CHANNEL_OPS`                      | Live ops channel ID.                                                                                                                          |
| `SLACK_CHANNEL_STAGING`                  | Sandbox/staging channel ID.                                                                                                                   |
| `SLACK_NOTIFICATIONS_ENABLED`            | Optional kill-switch. Defaults to enabled unless set to `false`.                                                                              |

No `STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY` is needed because we use hosted Checkout.

## Module Structure

`features/billing/` is split by responsibility:

- **Gateway** (`stripe.ts`) — Stripe SDK wrappers. No DB imports.
- **Customer** (`customer.ts`) — `ensureStripeCustomer` and trial eligibility. Customer bootstrap is the documented exception to webhook-only mirror writes because Stripe has no customer-created event that carries our org context.
- **Mirror** (`mirror.ts`) — narrow writers for `organization_billing`. Each writer can touch only its owned columns.
- **Sync** (`sync.ts`) — converts Stripe subscriptions into mirror snapshots and owns `mapStripeToBillingState`.
- **Policy** (`policy.ts`) — read-side enforcement helpers: the four intent-specific seat-policy entry points (`checkInviteCapacityForBatch`, `checkInviteAcceptance`, `checkScormProvisionCapacity`, `checkRolePromotionToMember`), entitlement reads, and a shared `computeSeatBudget` snapshot.
- **Locks** (`locks.ts`) — Postgres advisory locks for billing mutations and seat-consuming writes.
- **Webhook subscriptions** (`webhook-subscriptions.ts`) — subscription sync orchestration, duplicate-subscription defense, cancellation/refund path.
- **Reconciliation** (`reconciliation.ts`) — Stripe-vs-mirror drift classification, safe auto-correction, and unsafe drift alerts.
- **Slack notifications** (`slack-notifications.ts`) — billing-domain message builders and channel routing.

Adjacent entry points:

- `src/app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts`
- `src/app/api/cron/billing-reconciliation/route.ts`
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/`
- `src/lib/server/slack.ts`
- `src/lib/server/stripe.ts`

## Flows

### Initial subscription

The owner selects a seat count on the billing page. The action ensures the org has a Stripe Customer, checks live Stripe for an existing live subscription, determines local trial eligibility, and creates a Checkout Session with `payment_method_types: ["card"]`.

Checkout uses a short bucketed idempotency key based on org, seat count, trial policy, and a 30-second time bucket. This protects accidental retries without permanently blocking a legitimate new session attempt.

`subscription_data.metadata` carries the provisioning provenance: `organizationId`, `orgSlug`, `provisionedVia` (`'self_service'` or `'admin_launch'`), and `launchId` when applicable. The webhook reads metadata from the synced subscription rather than from `session.subscription_data` — the session field is configuration input, and by webhook time the metadata has propagated to the subscription, which is authoritative. The `admin_launch` value drives the preview→active lifecycle flip on `checkout.session.completed`. See `docs/provisioning.md`.

On completion, webhook events fetch the live subscription from Stripe and write the mirror. Trial usage is marked only when a Stripe subscription with `trial_end` is observed, never when a Checkout Session is merely opened.

### Seat increase

The `addSeats` action:

1. Holds the org seat lock.
2. Reads the local mirror and retrieves the live subscription.
3. Refuses canceled/non-live subscriptions, trial over-cap requests, and increases while `pendingSeatCount` exists.
4. Updates the Stripe subscription quantity.
5. Syncs the returned subscription into the mirror before route revalidation.

Active subscriptions use immediate proration. Trialing subscriptions use no proration.

### Seat reduction

The `reduceSeats` action validates that the requested count is a real reduction and is not below current learner count. The reduction is scheduled through a Stripe subscription schedule for the next cycle; local `seatCount` remains the current enforced cap and `pendingSeatCount` records the future count.

### Cancellation and resume

Cancellation sets `cancel_at_period_end: true`; access continues until the end date. Resume updates the same subscription with `cancel_at_period_end: false`. Both paths sync the returned subscription into the mirror immediately and post dedicated Slack signals (`Pending cancellation` and `Cancellation resumed`).

If a `subscription_schedule` is attached (i.e. a `reduceSeats` is pending for the next cycle), Stripe blocks direct subscription mutations. The cancel, resume, and add-seats flows release the schedule first; the future scheduled phase is dropped and the action layer clears `pendingSeatCount` so the mirror does not advertise a reduction that is no longer staged.

The final `customer.subscription.deleted` event writes one terminal cancellation snapshot: subscription fields, seat state, pending count, cancellation state, and entitlements are cleared together.

### Billing portal

The app passes `STRIPE_BILLING_PORTAL_CONFIGURATION_ID` when creating portal sessions. The portal is for payment method updates and invoice history. Pause, cancellation, plan changes, quantity changes, and async payment methods are disabled for pilot.

The portal assertion checks the live Dashboard configuration so the assumption is auditable from code, not only from Dashboard screenshots.

## Webhook Handler

Entry point: `src/app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts`

The handler uses `runtime = "nodejs"` because the Stripe SDK requires Node APIs.

### Core pattern

1. Verify the raw body with `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent`.
2. Insert the Stripe event ID into `stripe_processed_events`.
3. If the insert conflicts, return 200 without reprocessing.
4. Process the event and perform durable DB writes.
5. Emit Slack notifications after durable writes.
6. If processing fails, delete the processed marker so Stripe retries can reprocess the event.

The processed-event table dedupes mirror writes and Slack messages on Stripe retries. It does not replace idempotency keys on Stripe-side writes such as duplicate cancellation/refund.

### Handled events

| Event                                             | Action                                                                                                                                         |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `checkout.session.completed`                      | Fetch live subscription, sync mirror, post checkout Slack signal. When the synced subscription's metadata carries `provisionedVia='admin_launch'`, flip the org's `lifecycleState` from `preview` to `active`. Promotion failures propagate so Stripe retries the event. |
| `customer.subscription.created`                   | Fetch live subscription, sync mirror, mark trial usage if applicable. Generic Ops fallback is suppressed — richer signals (checkout completed, internal-attach) already cover this event. |
| `customer.subscription.updated`                   | Sync canonical subscription snapshot; post trial-converted, pending-cancel, cancellation-resumed, or seat-change signals when applicable.      |
| `customer.subscription.deleted`                   | Write atomic cancellation snapshot; post churn signal with Stripe cancellation reason when available.                                          |
| `customer.subscription.trial_will_end`            | Observability-only trial-ending Slack signal.                                                                                                  |
| `entitlements.active_entitlement_summary.updated` | Filter, dedupe, and sort known entitlement keys; write via `writeEntitlementSnapshot`. Paginates through Stripe when the summary is truncated. |
| `invoice.paid`                                    | Sync subscription, clear pending count if renewal applied, post invoice/trial-start/first-payment signal.                                      |
| `invoice.payment_failed`                          | Sync subscription and surface payment issue. No access restriction during pilot.                                                               |
| `invoice.payment_action_required`                 | Set `billingState = "requires_action"` and alert owner-facing recovery path.                                                                   |
| `invoice.finalization_failed`                     | Log and notify payment issue.                                                                                                                  |
| `charge.dispute.created`                          | Resolve org through charge/customer and post dispute alert.                                                                                    |
| `charge.dispute.closed`                           | Resolve org through charge/customer and post dispute resolution signal.                                                                        |

For subscription and invoice events, the handler fetches the live subscription from Stripe instead of trusting the event payload. Stripe event ordering and delivery are not guaranteed — see the duplicate-subscription defense below for why this matters.

### Duplicate subscriptions

The shared subscription sync path is the duplicate-defense gate. Any inbound subscription whose id mismatches the org's currently-mirrored live subscription is treated as a duplicate, regardless of which event surfaced it (`customer.subscription.created`, `checkout.session.completed`, or any of the `invoice.*` events). The check is gated on the *existing* mirror still pointing at a live status (`active`, `trialing`, `past_due`, `unpaid`, `incomplete`, `paused`); a canceled mirror falls through to a normal sync so reprovisioning is allowed.

The event-type-agnostic check is necessary because Stripe webhook delivery does not guarantee order. An `invoice.paid` event for a duplicate subscription can arrive before its `customer.subscription.created` event — without the order-tolerant guard, the early invoice would slip into the normal sync path and overwrite the mirror with the duplicate's data.

When the duplicate path fires:

1. Cancel the duplicate with an idempotency key.
2. Identify the latest invoice charge across current and legacy Stripe invoice shapes.
3. Refund with an idempotency key when the charge is confidently identified.
4. Otherwise post an ops alert for manual refund review.
5. Preserve the original mirrored subscription.

## Status Mapping

The sync layer maps Stripe subscription statuses into app billing states:

| Stripe status                      | App billingState |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------- |
| `active`                           | `active`         |
| `trialing`                         | `trialing`       |
| `past_due`, `unpaid`               | `past_due`       |
| `canceled`, `paused`               | `canceled`       |
| `incomplete`, `incomplete_expired` | `none`           |

Invoice action-required events can override the app state to `requires_action` until a later successful sync clears it.

## Reconciliation

The reconciliation cron makes the Stripe/mirror contract operational.

Route: `/api/cron/billing-reconciliation`

The route requires `Authorization: Bearer ${CRON_SECRET}` and fails closed if `CRON_SECRET` is missing. It derives live vs. sandbox routing from the Stripe secret key prefix because cron requests have no Stripe event payload.

Safe drift is auto-corrected, capped at 20 orgs per run. Unsafe drift posts Slack alerts and requires human review. Unsafe cases include:

- multiple live subscriptions for one org customer
- subscription with no organization
- unmirrored live subscription
- unknown price ID
- async payment method
- missing subscription item
- webhook endpoint version drift
- orphan scan truncation

The orphan scan lists subscriptions by configured pilot/internal price IDs and caps the scan at 1,000 subscriptions per price. If the cap is hit, reconciliation alerts instead of pretending the scan was complete.

If no price IDs are configured, reconciliation skips price-based unknown/orphan checks instead of flagging every subscription as unknown in local/dev environments.

## Slack Observability

Slack is observability only. It never owns app state and never blocks webhook processing.

`src/lib/server/slack.ts` is a direct `chat.postMessage` wrapper:

- no external Slack SDK
- no-op when disabled or missing token
- fire-and-forget
- logs Slack API errors
- never throws to callers

`src/features/billing/slack-notifications.ts` owns billing-specific messages. Routing uses Stripe `event.livemode` for webhook events:

- sandbox events go only to staging
- live events go to ops
- curated revenue events also go to revenue

Cron reconciliation has no event payload, so it derives live mode from `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`.

## Local Development

Install the Stripe CLI, log in, then forward webhooks to your dev server:

```
stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/api/stripe/webhook
```

Use the printed `whsec_…` value for `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`. Use Stripe Test Clocks to simulate trial ending, conversion, renewal, cancellation, and scheduled seat reductions without changing production trial logic.

When replaying a webhook event after `stripe_processed_events` is active, delete only that event's processed row or use a dedicated replay path. Do not disable dedupe globally.

## Related Docs

- `docs/billing/README.md` — billing model, seat enforcement, lifecycle rules
- `docs/billing/internal-plan.md` — internal entitlement plan and admin attach/detach
- `docs/provisioning.md` — admin-launch metadata, preview→active flip, deprovision billing-state guard
- `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-01-stripe-slack-notifications.md` — Slack operational setup
- `docs/action.md` — `orgOwnerActionClient`

# Internal Plan

> **Reference doc.** You don't need this for typical portal, lesson, or billing work. Read it when you're touching the `/admin` route, the internal subscription flow, or the `unlimited-*` entitlements. Most changes to the platform should leave everything described here alone.

The internal plan is a platform-admin-only escape hatch for QA'ing features that would otherwise be gated by billing (seat limits, PDF generation quota). It exists because the team ran out of clean ways to test the invite and generation flows end-to-end — forging DB rows, patching code locally, and running prod against sandbox Stripe all failed different ways. The internal plan replaces all three with a single, first-class mechanism.

## Mental model

Think of it as **"a dedicated Stripe product that, by existing on an org's customer, turns off specific enforcement gates in the platform"**. Three layered ideas:

1. **Stripe Entitlements are the primitive.** Stripe has a native concept of a named "feature" (e.g. `unlimited-seats`) that can be attached to a product. Any customer with an active subscription to that product automatically has that feature granted. This is a Stripe-side fact, not a platform-side boolean.
2. **The grant is delivered via a $0/month subscription.** Stripe's entitlements model requires subscription state — there's no "one-time grant" API. A $0 recurring price is the minimum viable subscription, renews monthly at no cost, generates no invoices that matter, and is indistinguishable from a one-time flag in practice.
3. **Platform code reads a local mirror, not Stripe.** The entitlements set is written to `organization_billing.entitlements` by a webhook handler, and every enforcement site reads the mirror. Stripe owns _what the org should have_; the mirror owns _what the app enforces at request time_. Bounded webhook lag (1–3 seconds in practice) is accepted in exchange for Stripe never being in the invitation or generation hot path.

## Why this shape, not something simpler

These were the real alternatives and why they lost:

- **A boolean column on `organization_billing`** (e.g. `bypass_seats: true`). Simplest to implement but couples enforcement to a hand-managed platform field. No audit trail in Stripe, no path to customer-facing entitlements later, no way to express "this org has X but not Y."
- **An env-var allowlist of org IDs.** Smallest diff but scatters business rules across code and deployment config. Every change requires a redeploy. Can't express multiple capabilities per org.
- **Customer metadata** (`is_internal: true`). Works but moves the boolean into Stripe without gaining anything — metadata has no semantic meaning and no query primitives.
- **A 100% coupon or infinite free trial.** Both are discount primitives, not capability primitives. They get you to $0 but can't answer "does this customer have feature X."
- **Stripe entitlements granted directly to a customer.** Sounds right; doesn't exist. Stripe's entitlement model strictly derives from subscription state. There is no customer-level grant API.

Entitlements attached to a $0 product is the only option that both fits Stripe's model _and_ gives you first-class capability semantics. If you ever need a second capability flag, you add a new feature to the same product and every subscribed org picks it up via webhook.

## What's in the plan today

Two features, both attached to the `iTELL Internal Plan` product:

| Feature lookup_key         | Bypasses                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `unlimited-seats`          | All seat-gate sites: invite creation, invitation acceptance, role changes into `member`, SCORM auto-provisioning, Better Auth defense-in-depth hooks, and the database seat triggers. Application checks go through the four entry points in `features/billing/policy.ts`; all four share a `computeSeatBudget` snapshot that short-circuits to `{ allowed: true }` when the entitlement is present, ahead of any headroom math. Triggers read the same mirrored entitlement. |
| `unlimited-pdf-generation` | The per-user generation quota check at both the upload form precheck and the `uploadPdf` mutation. The check is short-circuited for any user acting inside an org with the bypass — and critically, the counter is not incremented, so a developer's personal quota isn't silently burned while testing internally.                                               |

Adding a third capability is a three-step change: create the Stripe feature, attach it to the internal product, define a constant in `src/features/billing/entitlements.ts`. No schema change is required — the local mirror is already typed as `EntitlementKey[]`.

**Gotcha:** Stripe only propagates new feature attachments to existing subscriptions at their next billing period. For `$0` monthly subs, that's up to ~30 days of lag. If you need instant rollout, reprovision the affected internal orgs via the admin UI (detach then attach). The delete handler's defensive subscription-ID match guard is specifically designed to survive reprovision flows.

## The enforcement story

```
Invite/quota hot path (no Stripe dependency):
  action handler
    → seat-policy entry point (checkInviteCapacityForBatch /
      checkInviteAcceptance / checkScormProvisionCapacity /
      checkRolePromotionToMember) or orgHasEntitlement
    → computeSeatBudget snapshot
    → getOrganizationBilling           [local DB]
    → entitlements.includes('unlimited-seats') → allowed
    → otherwise: headroom check

Mirror population (out of band):
  Stripe event: entitlements.active_entitlement_summary.updated
    → webhook handler
    → resolve customer → organizationBilling row
    → filter via isEntitlementKey
    → dedupe + sort for canonical ordering
    → update organization_billing.entitlements

Detach tombstone:
  Stripe event: customer.subscription.deleted
    → match subscription.id against mirrored stripeSubscriptionId (defensive)
    → clear entitlements along with seat/subscription fields

Seat-trigger bypass:
  members / pending invitations trigger
    → lock organization_billing row
    → entitlements includes unlimited-seats
    → allow without requiring a seat count
```

The mirror is the _only_ reader path for enforcement. The "entitlements column has exactly two writers" rule is enforced by types: `billing/mirror.ts` exposes `writeEntitlementSnapshot` (used by the entitlement-summary webhook) and `writeCancellationSnapshot` (used by the `subscription.deleted` handler to clear the column atomically with the rest of the terminal-state snapshot — splitting these into separate writes opened a partial-failure window where a canceled org could keep `unlimited-*` entitlements until Stripe retried). All other mirror writers, including `writeSubscriptionSnapshot` used by `billing/sync.ts`, have parameter types that do not include `entitlements` — so a future change that tries to touch the column from anywhere else is a compile error, not a race. The defensive subscription-ID match on `subscription.deleted` still applies, so a stale out-of-order delete during a reprovision can't clobber fresh state.

## The `planKind` classifier

A related concept the billing UI and the `/admin` route both depend on: classifying an org's billing row by plan.

`getPlanKind(billing)` reads `billing.stripePriceId` and returns one of:

- `"none"` — no subscription
- `"pilot"` — on the paying pilot price (`STRIPE_PILOT_PRICE_ID`)
- `"internal"` — on the internal price (`STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`)
- `"unknown"` — has a `stripePriceId` that matches neither env var

`"unknown"` is deliberate and load-bearing. An org with a subscription on an unrecognized price (legacy grandfathered price, manually-created sub, typo'd env var) must never fall through to `"none"` — that would render the "Get Started" checkout UI and risk creating a second subscription on top of an active one. Billing page and admin UI both render `"unknown"` as read-only, and the server logs a warning so operators can investigate.

Entry points: `src/features/billing/plan-kind.ts`, `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/page.tsx` (branches for `"internal"` and `"unknown"`), `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/_components/internal-plan-card.tsx`, `…/unknown-plan-card.tsx`.

## The `/admin` route

Platform admins (`user.role === "admin"`, Better Auth admin plugin) have a top-level, flat `/admin` route — not inside the `(portal)` shell. It exists specifically because the internal plan needs a UI somewhere to attach and detach, and that UI cannot live inside an org scope (it operates across all orgs).

Auth is enforced by a new action client:

`authActionClient → platformAdminActionClient`

`platformAdminActionClient` extends `authActionClient` (not `orgActionClient`) because its routes are not org-scoped. It checks `ctx.user.role === "admin"` and injects `ctx.isPlatformAdmin`. The `/admin/layout.tsx` uses the same role check + `forbidden()` to gate UI rendering.

### Why it's not inside `(portal)`

The portal layout resolves an active org from the URL slug and fetches billing state for the sidebar. `/admin` has no org in the URL, so forcing it through the portal layout would either need defensive no-op branches (messy) or a separate sidebar (pointless — `/admin` already has its own minimal header). A flat top-level route is cheaper and cleaner.

### The attach/detach flow

`/admin/organizations` is the only surface. The server component lists every org joined with its billing row and classifies each via `getPlanKind`. The client component offers:

- `"none"` → `Attach Internal` primary button
- `"internal"` → `Detach Internal` destructive action with `AlertDialog` confirmation
- `"pilot"` → disabled, tooltip explains why (paying customer, can't displace)
- `"unknown"` → disabled, tooltip explains why (unrecognized subscription, investigate manually)

The actions (`attachInternalPlan`, `detachInternalPlan`) are not simple wrappers over the Stripe helpers — they're deliberately defensive against mirror staleness and concurrency:

**Attach path:**

1. Fast-path mirror refusals for `"pilot"` and `"unknown"` (fail-safe direction — false positives are annoying but not dangerous).
2. Load org + owner context, ensure a Stripe customer exists.
3. **Live Stripe check**: list the customer's active subscriptions directly. If an internal sub already exists, return idempotent success (the mirror was lagging — the webhook will catch up on its own). If any non-internal sub exists, refuse. This is the actual guardrail against duplicates, not the mirror.
4. Create the subscription with a stable per-org idempotency key (`internal-sub:${organizationId}`) so two concurrent clicks converge on a single subscription inside Stripe's 24h dedupe window.

**Detach path:**

1. Fast-path mirror refusals for `"pilot"` and `"unknown"`.
2. **Live Stripe retrieve** on the mirrored `stripeSubscriptionId`. If the sub is already canceled, return idempotent success. If it's not the internal price, refuse — the mirror was lying, and we never want to cancel the wrong sub.
3. Cancel immediately (no `cancel_at_period_end` — it's a $0 plan, nothing to prorate).

Neither handler writes to `organization_billing` directly. The webhook pipeline is the sole writer, consistent with the mirror-source-of-truth rule for enforcement.

### Deleting an internal-plan org

Normal pilot subscriptions block organization deletion until the subscription has ended. Internal-plan orgs are different because the subscription is a $0 platform-admin bypass. The org deletion action verifies the live subscription's first item price against `STRIPE_INTERNAL_PRICE_ID`, cancels the internal subscription immediately, then deletes the org locally without waiting for the cancellation webhook.

The late `customer.subscription.deleted` webhook can no-op safely because the local billing row has already been removed by cascade. This keeps Stripe clean without holding a destructive org deletion open on asynchronous webhook delivery.

### Pending-state UX

Because writes are webhook-driven, the admin table can't just `revalidatePath` once and be done — the action returns before the webhook arrives. The client component instead:

- Tracks per-row optimistic state (`attaching` / `removing`) with a `Map<orgId, { action, startedAt }>`.
- Polls `router.refresh()` every 1 second while any row is pending.
- Exits the pending state when the server re-renders with the expected `planKind` for that row, or after a 10-second timeout (fires a warning toast).

The 10-second timeout is a liveness guarantee, not a hard deadline — normal webhook latency is 1–3 seconds. If the timeout fires, operators are told to refresh manually and investigate.

## One-time Stripe setup

The internal product, features, and price are provisioned by a dedicated idempotent script: `scripts/stripe-setup-internal.ts`. It creates or finds (by `lookup_key` for features/prices, by `metadata.itell_key = "internal"` for the product) and safely no-ops on re-run. Does not touch the pilot catalog — that's still dashboard-managed, to avoid duplicate products if the script's metadata tag differs from what an operator chose manually.

Separately, the webhook endpoint must be subscribed to `entitlements.active_entitlement_summary.updated` in the Stripe dashboard. Without that event, attach calls succeed but the local mirror never populates and nothing is bypassed.

## Critical files

- `src/features/billing/entitlements.ts` — `ENTITLEMENT_KEYS`, `EntitlementKey`, `ENTITLEMENT_LABELS`, `isEntitlementKey`
- `src/features/billing/plan-kind.ts` — `PlanKind`, `getPlanKind`
- `src/features/billing/policy.ts` — `getOrgEntitlements`, `orgHasEntitlement`, and the four seat-policy entry points (the only enforcement-side readers of the entitlements column)
- `src/features/billing/locks.ts` — org-level advisory locks for seat and billing mutation serialization
- `src/features/billing/mirror.ts` — narrow column-locked writers; `writeEntitlementSnapshot` and `writeCancellationSnapshot` are the only two functions whose parameter types include `entitlements`
- `drizzle-migrations/0010_seat-enforcement-triggers.sql` — database backstop for learner members and pending learner invitations
- `src/features/billing/customer.ts` — `ensureStripeCustomer` (the documented gateway+mirror composition exception)
- `src/features/billing/stripe.ts` — `createInternalSubscription`, `cancelInternalSubscription`, `listCustomerSubscriptions`, `getInternalPriceId` (pure SDK gateway, no DB)
- `src/app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts` — `entitlements.active_entitlement_summary.updated` handler (uses `Stripe.Entitlements.ActiveEntitlementSummary` directly) and `customer.subscription.deleted` handler (uses `writeCancellationSnapshot`)
- `src/app/admin/layout.tsx`, `src/app/admin/organizations/page.tsx`, `…/actions.ts`, `…/_components/org-admin-table.tsx`
- `src/lib/action-client.ts` — `platformAdminActionClient`
- `scripts/stripe-setup-internal.ts` — one-time provisioning

## Related docs

- `docs/billing/README.md` — billing mental model, seat enforcement, access model
- `docs/billing/stripe-integration.md` — webhook handler, object mapping, env vars
- `docs/action.md` — action client chain, `platformAdminActionClient`

# Customer Launch Provisioning

Provisioning is the admin-driven path that creates an organization before any billing relationship exists. It's the alternative to self-service `authClient.organization.create()` for sales-led customers: iTELL stages the org, owner accepts an invitation, and the customer drives activation themselves.

## Mental Model

Think of an org as having a **lifecycle state** in addition to its billing state:

- `lifecycleState = 'preview'` — provisioned org. Owner and admins can sign in and inspect content, but learners are blocked at the seat gate and no Stripe customer exists.
- `lifecycleState = 'active'` — normal mode. Learner onboarding follows the standard billing checks.

Lifecycle state expresses **product mode**, not Stripe state. A `preview` org has no subscription at all; a `past_due` org is `active` in lifecycle and `past_due` in billing. The two are orthogonal.

Self-service orgs start at `active`. Admin-provisioned orgs start at `preview` and flip to `active` when the customer completes activation Checkout.

## Why Provisioning Exists

Better Auth's organization plugin forces the creator to be the owner. That couples three identities — provisioner, owner, and payer — that are distinct in a sales-led motion. The provisioning service writes Better-Auth-compatible rows directly under `platformAdminActionClient`, then the customer's owner becomes owner through the normal invitation acceptance hook. The acting platform admin is recorded for audit but is not made a member of the resulting org.

## Customer Launches

`customer_launches` is a minimal entity with two states:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `draft` | Form being filled. No org exists yet. Editable. |
| `submitted` | Provisioning ran. Org exists. The row stays as audit. |

All other "lifecycle" concerns live on the org. The launch row carries the inputs (org name, slug, owner email, admin emails, bundled Payload volumes, trial policy, optional agreed seat count), a `configHash` for idempotency, and snapshot columns (`resultingOrgIdSnapshot`, `resultingOrgSlugSnapshot`) so the launch remains a complete audit record after deprovisioning hard-deletes the org.

Snapshot columns are plain text, not foreign keys. The launch is the audit anchor; the org row is the operational one. They reference each other through soft links, not cascades, so deprovisioning can hard-delete the org without losing identity from the audit trail.

## End-to-End Flow

```
draft         submit            checkout completed
  |             |                       |
  v             v                       v
[draft]    [submitted]              [submitted]
            org=preview              org=active
            invitations              invitations accepted
            sent
```

1. **Draft.** Platform admin composes the launch in `/admin/launches/new`: org name, slug, owner email, additional admin emails, one or more Payload volumes, trial policy (`none` or `standard_30d`), optional `agreedSeatCount`.
2. **Submit.** `provisionOrganization` re-validates inputs against current state (slug not taken, owner email not in admin list, each Payload volume resolves) and opens a Drizzle transaction. In the transaction: insert `organizations` with `lifecycleState='preview'` and `trialPolicy` copied from the launch; per bundled volume, insert one `teams` row and one `organization_volumes` row with `teamId` populated; insert invitations (owner with `role='owner' accessScope='all'`, admins with `role='admin'`); mark the launch `submitted` with snapshots and `configHash`. Emails fire post-commit; failures are non-fatal because resend is available.
3. **Owner accepts.** Better Auth's `afterAcceptInvitation` hook runs normally — direct-inserted invitation rows are indistinguishable from plugin-created ones at acceptance time. The owner lands in the preview org with full access.
4. **Activation.** Owner clicks "Activate billing" → `activatePreviewBilling` opens hosted Checkout with `subscription_data.metadata.provisionedVia = 'admin_launch'` and the launch ID. On `checkout.session.completed`, the webhook reads the synced subscription's metadata, recognizes the admin-launch path, and flips lifecycle to `active`.

Re-submission of an already-submitted launch is a no-op when the inputs match the original `configHash` (returns the snapshot). Mismatched hash is refused — the draft was edited after submission and the difference must be resolved deliberately.

## Where the Gate Lives

Preview blocking is centralized in the shared `computeSeatBudget` snapshot inside `features/billing/policy.ts`. It short-circuits with `{ allowed: false, reason: 'preview' }` ahead of the entitlement and subscription checks. That single snapshot covers all four intent-specific seat-policy entry points (`checkInviteCapacityForBatch`, `checkInviteAcceptance`, `checkScormProvisionCapacity`, `checkRolePromotionToMember`) — and through them, every learner-onboarding path (bulk invite, invitation acceptance, role-change-into-member, user-initiated acceptance, SCORM auto-provision, and the two Better Auth defense-in-depth hooks). There is no separate gate at the invite form or layout.

## What Each Org State Allows

Lifecycle and billing are independent axes, so an org can be in several states. The practical "what works right now" matrix:

| Action                                   | Preview (admin-launched)                          | Active, no subscription                     | Active, trialing or paid           |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Owner / admin signs in, inspects content | ✅                                                | ✅                                          | ✅                                 |
| Invite additional admin or owner         | ✅ no billing gate                                | ✅ no billing gate                          | ✅ no billing gate                 |
| Invite learner                           | ❌ refused: `preview`                             | ❌ refused: `billing_required`              | ✅ up to `seatCount`               |
| Learner accepts invitation               | ❌ refused: `preview`                             | ❌ refused: `billing_required`              | ✅ up to `seatCount`               |
| Role-change → `member`                   | ❌ refused: `preview`                             | ❌ refused: `billing_required`              | ✅ up to `seatCount`               |
| SCORM auto-provision learner             | ❌ refused: `preview`                             | ❌ refused: `billing_required`              | ✅ up to `seatCount`               |
| Activate billing                         | "Activate billing" CTA → `activatePreviewBilling` | "Get started" CTA → `createCheckoutSession` | n/a                                |
| Add seats                                | n/a                                               | n/a                                         | ✅ owner only                      |
| Reduce seats                             | n/a                                               | n/a                                         | ✅ owner only, next-cycle schedule |

Two asymmetries that drive most of the surprises:

- **Admins are unmetered everywhere.** Owner/admin invites bypass the seat policy entirely — free regardless of lifecycle or subscription state. Enforced at both the application layer (`if (role === "member")` gate in `inviteWithVolumes`) and the DB trigger layer (`enforce_member_seat_limit` early-returns on non-member rows). The supported workaround for "give learners free access" is *not* to invite them as admins — see the anti-pattern note in `docs/billing/README.md`.
- **Preview shadows everything else.** The lifecycle check runs first inside `computeSeatBudget`, ahead of entitlement and subscription checks, so even an `unlimited-seats` entitlement does not unblock learner onboarding in a preview org — the lifecycle has to flip to `active` first.

**Trial vs preview is not the same axis.** Preview gives admins free content access while learner onboarding stays blocked. A trial (set via `trialPolicy='standard_30d'` at launch) gives the org real student usage with no charge for ~30 days but requires a card on file at activation. They serve different sales motions: preview is "let admins evaluate the content"; trial is "let the customer pilot with real students before paying."

## Activation Path

Activation Checkout is a distinct action from self-service Checkout:

- `createCheckoutSession` — self-service. Refuses `lifecycleState='preview'` so the wrong subscription metadata can't be painted on a preview org.
- `activatePreviewBilling` — preview-only. Reads the org's `trialPolicy` and `provisionedFromLaunchId`, stamps `provisionedVia='admin_launch'` and `launchId` onto `subscription_data.metadata`.

Both share `buildCheckoutSubscriptionData` and `trialParamsForCheckout` (the pure mapper that translates `'none' | 'standard_30d'` into Stripe's `trial_period_days` parameter).

The webhook reads metadata from the **synced subscription**, not from `session.subscription_data`. The session field is configuration input; by the time the webhook fires, that metadata has propagated to the subscription, which is authoritative.

Lifecycle promotion is idempotent on retries (`UPDATE … WHERE lifecycleState='preview'`). Promotion failures propagate — the outer webhook handler deletes the processed-event marker so Stripe retries the event. A failed flip must never become a silent 200, otherwise an org sits paid-but-preview indefinitely.

## Audit FK Anchoring

The provisioning service writes rows with two distinct user identities:

- **Cascading FKs** (`organization_volumes.created_by_id`, `invitations.inviter_id`) are anchored to a durable system user (`users.id = 'system:provisioning'`, seeded by migration). Using the acting admin's userId would let admin deletion cascade through customer rows.
- **Audit FKs** (`organizations.provisioned_by_actor_id`, `customer_launches.drafted_by_user_id`, `customer_launches.deprovisioned_by_actor_id`) point at the real actor and use `ON DELETE SET NULL`. The audit row survives admin deletion with the actor reduced to `null`.

The acting admin's name still surfaces on the launch detail page through these audit columns.

## Deprovisioning

Deprovision is the path for deals that fall through before activation. It hard-deletes the `organizations` row (cascading volumes, teams, pending invitations) and stamps `deprovisioned_at`, `deprovisioned_by_actor_id`, `deprovision_reason` on the launch row. Snapshot identity is preserved.

Guards, in order:

1. Launch must exist and be `submitted`, not already deprovisioned, with snapshots present.
2. Slug confirmation must match the snapshot — the admin types the slug to confirm.
3. The org must still be in `lifecycleState='preview'`.
4. `organization_billing` must be terminal (`billingState IN ('none', 'canceled')` and no `stripeSubscriptionId`).

The fourth guard closes a window: between `checkout.session.completed` and the webhook lifecycle flip, lifecycle is still `preview` while a Stripe subscription exists. Deleting the org row there would cascade `organization_billing` away while leaving the Stripe subscription alive and billing the customer forever. Abandoned-Checkout customers (Stripe customer exists, no subscription, `billingState='none'`) are still safe to deprovision.

If the org row was already deleted externally but the launch snapshot exists, deprovision stamps the audit row and exits — idempotent against drift.

Active or canceled orgs are not deprovisioned; they follow the normal cancellation + org-deletion flow described in `docs/billing/README.md`.

## Launch Invitations

Owner/admin launch invitations bypass two pieces of the standard invitation flow:

- **No `invitation_volumes` rows.** Owners and admins implicitly access every org volume via `accessScope='all'`. Writing per-invite scope rows for them would overload a learner-scope table.
- **No Better Auth `createInvitation` call.** The plugin's typed surface rejects `role: 'owner'`. The provisioning service inserts the invitation row directly inside the same transaction as the org and teams; Better Auth's `afterAcceptInvitation` hook works against the resulting row exactly as it would for plugin-created rows.

`sendLaunchInvitationEmail` is a launch-flavored variant of the org invitation email that takes course names directly (resolved from the launch's `volumeImports`) instead of joining `invitation_volumes`.

### Resend

The launch detail page surfaces pending invitations with a per-row Resend action. `resendLaunchInvitationForId`:

1. Verifies the invitation belongs to a submitted, non-deprovisioned launch.
2. Allows resend for stored `status='pending'` regardless of effective status — including expired rows. Owner-role invites are unrecoverable through the org-admin Send Invitations flow (Better Auth's `createInvitation` rejects `role='owner'`), so refresh-and-resend inside the launch service is the only recovery path. Terminal stored statuses (`accepted`, `canceled`, `rejected`) stay refused.
3. Refreshes `expiresAt` with a direct UPDATE (same compatibility contract as the direct insert at provisioning), then sends the email.

## Surfaces

| Surface | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/admin/launches` | List view: drafts to continue, submitted launches with links to resulting orgs and lifecycle state. |
| `/admin/launches/new` | New-draft form: org name, slug, owner email, admin emails, Payload volume picker (reuses the catalog pattern from `/admin/public-volumes`), trial policy, agreed seat count. |
| `/admin/launches/[id]` | Edit for drafts; read-only summary + pending-invitations panel + deprovision affordance for submitted launches. |
| `/org/[orgSlug]/billing` | Reads `getOrgProvisioningContext` and renders preview-flavored copy + the `activatePreviewBilling` CTA pre-filled with `agreedSeatCount` when the org is in `preview`. |

`/admin/launches/*` is gated by `platformAdminActionClient` like the other `/admin/*` surfaces.

## Critical Files

- `src/db/schemas/customer-launches.schema.ts` — the launch table
- `src/db/schemas/auth.schema.ts` — `organizations.lifecycleState` and provisioning audit columns
- `src/features/provisioning/service.ts` — `provisionOrganization` orchestration
- `src/features/provisioning/provision-tx.ts` — transactional row writes
- `src/features/provisioning/hash.ts` / `validate.ts` — pure inputs
- `src/features/provisioning/lifecycle.ts` — `promotePreviewToActive`
- `src/features/provisioning/deprovision.ts` — deprovision guards and transaction
- `src/features/provisioning/launch-resend.ts` — hardened resend
- `src/features/provisioning/launch-emails.ts` — launch-flavored invitation email
- `src/features/provisioning/system-user.ts` — `SYSTEM_PROVISIONING_USER_ID` constant
- `src/features/billing/policy.ts` — `computeSeatBudget` preview short-circuit (shared by all four seat-policy entry points)
- `src/features/billing/checkout-params.ts` / `trial-policy.ts` — shared Checkout shape
- `src/app/admin/launches/*` — admin surface
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/actions.ts` — `activatePreviewBilling`
- `src/app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts` — `maybeFlipPreviewLifecycle`
- `drizzle-migrations/0017_provisioning_system_user.sql` — system-user seed

## Known Limitations and Follow-ups

Carried forward from the multi-agent review that landed the lifecycle hardening. None of these are production blockers; they're recorded here so the next person touching the area inherits the full picture.

### Data / lifecycle

- **Stripe Customer objects orphan on deprovision.** `activatePreviewBilling` runs `ensureStripeCustomer` before redirecting to Checkout. If the customer abandons Checkout and the launch is deprovisioned, the local `organization_billing` row is cascade-deleted but the Stripe `Customer` object remains. Customers are free in Stripe and produce no billing events, so this is leak-only, not money-flow. Cleanup would call Stripe's customer-delete from within the deprovision transaction.
- **`cancelPendingInvitationsOnReInvite` leaks through the sentinel-user story.** Provisioned admin invitations point `inviter_id` at `system:provisioning` (cascade-safe). If a platform admin later re-invites the same email via the standard org-admin Send Invitations flow, Better Auth's auto-cancel supersedes the original row with a new row whose `inviter_id` is the acting admin — deleting that admin would cascade-delete the new invitation. Edge case in practice; documented for runbook awareness.
- **Sentinel user has no exclusion filters yet.** `system:provisioning` will appear in any future "list all users" surface, analytics aggregate, or impersonation picker. No such surface exists today, so this is preventive. A single helper that returns the standard `WHERE users.id != 'system:provisioning'` predicate is the natural shape when the first surface lands.

### Performance

- **Invitation emails send serially in `provisionOrganization`.** Each `sendLaunchInvitationEmail` awaits the previous; with five admin emails and a rate-limited email provider, the action serializes ~5x the per-email latency. Switching to `Promise.allSettled` is non-blocking, but the launch detail page's resend button is the recovery path so the current shape is acceptable.

### Resend edge cases

- **`resendLaunchInvitationForId` has a tiny race with deprovision.** Between the launch-existence check and the email send, a concurrent deprovision can cascade-delete the invitation row. The email send's `if (!row) return;` short-circuits silently while the action returns success — the admin sees an "Invitation re-sent" toast for a row that's gone. Race window is small; surfacing a "no longer exists" outcome upstream would close it.
- **`resendLaunchInvitationForId` returns the refreshed `expiresAt` but the action discards it.** The detail page could surface "expires in 7 days" after a resend; today it shows no feedback beyond the success toast.

### UI polish

- **`DeprovisionButton`'s reset races the dialog close animation.** `setSlug("")` fires the same tick as `setOpen(false)`; the typed slug visibly disappears during the ~150ms exit animation. Reset on the next open instead, or `onAnimationEnd` of the content.
- **`PendingInvitationsPanel` lacks an overflow wrapper.** Four-column table inside a card with no `overflow-x-auto`; narrow viewports will horizontal-scroll the card instead of the table. Admin surfaces aren't mobile-first, so this is low priority.
- **`LaunchTable` doesn't truncate long org names.** Pathological inputs blow out the table on small screens. `truncate` + `min-w-0` on the parent is the standard fix.
- **`PayloadVolumeTable`'s loading spinner has redundant aria.** `role="status" aria-live="polite"` wraps an icon with `aria-label="Loading…"` — screen readers announce twice. Drop one.
- **Sticky CTA bar has no visual elevation cue when content scrolls under it.** A `border-t` is the only separator; a 1px top shadow when scrolled would be clearer.

### Tooling not present yet

- **Stale-preview reporting.** A launch where the owner accepted the invitation but never activated billing sits in preview indefinitely. There's no surface for "preview orgs older than N days with no Checkout activity" — operations could plausibly need this to nudge customers stuck mid-onboarding. Filter on `organizations.lifecycle_state='preview'` joined with launch age.

## Related Docs

- `docs/organization.md` — invitation acceptance hook, owner/admin roles, org creation paths
- `docs/billing/README.md` — seat enforcement, lifecycle rules, org deletion (active orgs)
- `docs/billing/stripe-integration.md` — Checkout metadata convention, webhook event handling
- `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-13-customer-launch-provisioning.md` — design spec

Output:
## Project Overview

This is a Next.js 16 TypeScript project using the App Router and Tailwind CSS v4. It serves as a SaaS-style learning management platform by the company iTELL. Customers are represented as organizations, who upload or import content to produce interactive learning volumes.

## Mental Model

The platform owns **tenancy, access, billing, and learner analytics**. Payload owns **content**. Three nouns dominate:

- The **organization** — tenant boundary and billing unit.
- The **organization-volume binding** — per-org pointer to content, carrying its own access team, status, and analytics.
- The **Payload volume** — content itself, addressed by `cmsDocumentId`.

Every feature composes from these three. For a topic-scoped deep dive at the start of a session, invoke `/onboarding <topic phrase>`.

## Key Invariants

These rules cross subsystem boundaries. Older code may contradict them; trust the invariants.

1. **The organization is the tenant boundary, the billing unit, and the root of access.** One Stripe customer = one org; one org has at most one active subscription. Org membership is what grants workspace entry; everything below it keys off it. Don't introduce primitives that scope below or above this boundary.
2. **`organizationVolumeId` is the platform key for org-scoped state** — not slug, not `cmsDocumentId`. Progress, activity, completion, lesson time, dashboards all key off this binding. The binding row is the join point between tenancy, access, generation, and analytics; it must stay stable and first-class.
3. **Volume access and seat billing are independent axes.** A learner consumes one seat regardless of how many volumes they can open. `members.role = "member"` projects to billing; `team_members` projects to volume access. Code that assumes "seat purchased → all volumes auto-granted" is pre-decoupling.
4. **Route groups are authorization tiers, not just layout containers.** `(admin)` enforces admin/owner; `(owner)` is owner-only; `(lesson)` enforces team membership for non-admins; `/scorm/*` lives outside the tree due to iframe constraints. Adding a page means picking the right group.
5. **Privileged writes go through the action middleware chain.** `auth → org → {orgAdmin | orgOwner | volume} || platformAdmin`. Each layer adds one trust fact. The volume-access policy in `src/lib/server/volume-access-policy.ts` is shared with route handlers that can't use next-safe-action. Don't re-implement membership checks inside handlers.
6. **Content flows as a pipeline: Payload → markdown/remark → custom directives → React.** Payload is the source of truth; the platform reads tolerantly (per-block parsing, `label` fallback) and never mirrors content into Postgres. Touching content reads or rendering means knowing which layer owns the step.
7. **Lesson-runtime writes must be transactionally coherent.** Activity + page-completion + streak rows are coupled atomically. The reading-sinks abstraction lets the same components serve org and public surfaces by swapping the persistence sink — components never import actions directly.
8. **Tracking is a property of the URL, not the visitor.** `/explore/*` is non-tracking even for admins: no learner records, no progress writes, localStorage-only. The hardcoded catalog at `features/public-volumes/catalog.ts` is the trust boundary.

## Conventions

- Use kebab-case for file and folder names
- Use PascalCase for React component names
- Keep comments minimal and concise, document the intention behind complex logic
- Avoid explicit return types unless representing domain models or complex generics
- **DO NOT** create barrel files for re-exporting; import directly from individual modules
- `@/*` maps to `./src/*`, prefer `@/` imports
- Use `…` (ellipsis character) not `...` — including loading states: "Loading…", "Saving…"
- Handle empty states — never render broken UI for empty strings/arrays

## Next.js 16 Patterns

### Async Request APIs

In Next.js 16, request-scoped APIs (`cookies()`, `headers()`, `params`, `searchParams`) are asynchronous. Always `await` them:

```tsx
// Server Component
export default async function Page({
  params,
  searchParams,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
  searchParams: Promise<{ query?: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const { query } = await searchParams;
  const [headersList, cookieStore] = await Promise.all([headers(), cookies()]);
}
```

### Navigation Functions

These throw internally—call outside try/catch blocks:

| Function | Purpose | Renders |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `redirect(url)` | Redirect to another page | N/A (throws) |
| `notFound()` | Render 404 page | `not-found.tsx` |

## Design & UI

All design, styling, component, accessibility, animation, and Tailwind v4 rules live in **`docs/design.md`**. Read that before writing UI — it covers brand context, the semantic token system (`bg-success`, `bg-destructive`, etc.), shadcn + Base UI conventions (`render` vs `asChild`), Tailwind v4 renames, the `motion/react-m` import rule, and the anti-pattern list flagged in review.

Quick reminders for any UI work:

- `cn()` utility from `@/lib/utils` for conditional classes
- Use `@/components/ui` shadcn primitives before creating new components
- Semantic tokens only — never raw color scales (`bg-green-500`) for state
- Import `m` from `motion/react-m`, not `motion` from `motion/react`
- Animation respects `prefers-reduced-motion` (CSS `motion-reduce:` or JS `useReducedMotion`)

## Agent skills

### Issue tracker

Issues live in GitHub Issues on `learlab/itell` (use the `gh` CLI). See `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md`.

### Triage labels

Canonical defaults (`needs-triage`, `needs-info`, `ready-for-agent`, `ready-for-human`, `wontfix`). See `docs/agents/triage-labels.md`.

### Domain docs

Single-context. Domain vocabulary lives in the `docs/` tree (no separate `CONTEXT.md`); load-bearing decisions live in `docs/adr/`. See `docs/agents/domain.md`.

Completed the onboarding pass for auth, org roles, access control, and billing.

Load-bearing model I’ll use for future work in this area:

- **Organization is the boundary**: tenant, Stripe customer, billing unit, and workspace access root.
- **Roles are split**: `owner` has full org + billing control; `admin` manages volumes, people, dashboards, and settings; `member` is the learner role and the only seat-consuming org role.
- **Access and billing are separate**: `members.role = "member"` consumes seats; `team_members` controls which organization-volume bindings a learner can open.
- **Admins/owners bypass volume team checks**, but still need org membership. Platform admins are a separate `users.role === "admin"` concept and only bypass where the action middleware explicitly allows it.
- **Billing is owner-only** through `orgOwnerActionClient`; seat checks are centralized in `src/features/billing/policy.ts`.
- **Seat policy counts active learner members plus live pending learner invitations**, and all learner-onboarding paths use the same four budget entry points.
- **Preview orgs block learner onboarding before billing checks**, even before entitlement checks.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
I received this following github issue titled "additional permissions for org admins"

full content is

```
Currently, only owners can manage billing and add seats (we think). A user was an admin and expected the ability to start subscription and add seats, but was unable to. Additionally there was no way to promote the admin user to an owner or transfer ownership to her.

Owners should be able to grant permissions to admins, or promote admins to an intermediary role. Let's discuss the details.

Owners should be able to transfer ownership.
```

can you verify our existing implementation based on this statement, whether it is 100% true or not, analyze what would be a reasonable UX/UI improvement to make.

I don't think we are going to add a new role such as billing, keep the current "owner" / "admin" / "user" divide, but the need for admin to maintain billing is real, Because usually the owner is someone at the upper level and the real management is handled by the admin. For example, in a course, the owner is usually the professor, but the person actually sending out invitations and managing people is the TA, who acts as the admin.

I think it makes sense to give admins the power to do things, but it also gives us time to reflect on the true difference between an owner and an admin. Right now, we don't have many customers, and those we do have often have a contract or a deal with us before they even purchase seats. Because of that, we don't need to be over-cautious about admins managing billing or transferring ownership. I don't think it makes much of a difference who the owner is at this stage; just keep that notion in mind.

You probably need to use Contact Seven to find some relevant patterns for ownership transfer. While the state update itself is simple, it probably needs some kind of verification chain to really confirm the transfer of ownership.

---

1. State the real problem and the underlying UX goal in your own words. If my framing seems off, push back before going further.
2. Evaluate the current design against that goal — where does it fall short, and is the issue local or structural?
