     1	import "server-only";
     2	
     3	import { and, eq, lt } from "drizzle-orm";
     4	
     5	import { db } from "@/db";
     6	import { ownershipTransfers } from "@/db/schemas/ownership-transfers.schema";
     7	
     8	type DbTransaction = Parameters<Parameters<typeof db.transaction>[0]>[0];
     9	
    10	// Lazy expiry sweep: stored `pending` rows past their expiresAt are flipped
    11	// to `expired` at the action boundary so the partial unique index sees
    12	// accurate state. Mirrors cancelExpiredPendingInvitations's placement.
    13	//
    14	// The optional tx parameter lets the accept path run the sweep inside its
    15	// own custody transaction (same tx that holds the advisory lock) instead
    16	// of going through the pool — important because the accept path's
    17	// expired-on-accept branch needs the row update to be atomic with the
    18	// later transactional decisions.
    19	export async function markExpiredPendingTransfers(
    20	  organizationId: string,
    21	  tx?: DbTransaction,
    22	) {
    23	  const executor = tx ?? db;
    24	  await executor
    25	    .update(ownershipTransfers)
    26	    .set({ status: "expired" })
    27	    .where(
    28	      and(
    29	        eq(ownershipTransfers.organizationId, organizationId),
    30	        eq(ownershipTransfers.status, "pending"),
    31	        lt(ownershipTransfers.expiresAt, new Date()),
    32	      ),
    33	    );
    34	}

docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:9:The platform encodes "owner" as the only role with billing rights — checkout, seat changes, portal access, cancellation, resume. Admins, who in practice run the day-to-day workflow (invitations, content, dashboards, settings, and even org deletion), cannot unblock learner onboarding when seats run out. The reported case was a TA who needed to add seats while the professor (owner) was unreachable, and there was no way to promote the TA to owner or transfer ownership either.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:42:3. **Action-client chain is the policy layer.** The change is to swap `orgOwnerActionClient` → `orgAdminActionClient` on the eight billing actions, and keep `orgOwnerActionClient` for the new owner-only operations (transfer initiate/cancel, delete org). Transfer **acceptance** runs under `authActionClient` because the recipient is the actor, not the gate target. The middleware definitions in `src/lib/action-client.ts` are not restructured.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:43:4. **Route group moves from `(owner)` to `(admin)`.** The billing folder is physically relocated: `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/` → `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/billing/`. The `(owner)` route group itself is removed once its only inhabitant moves out — preventing dead policy surface in the route tree.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:50:11. **Stripe customer email is the billing contact, set by the actor.** The reviewer's pragmatic framing wins here: Stripe `Customer.email` is functionally a billing contact (where receipts go), not a custody record. Whoever triggers Checkout becomes the initial billing contact, and the email is editable by any admin or owner via the Stripe Portal. We rename `ensureStripeCustomer`'s `ownerEmail` parameter to `billingContactEmail` to make this honest, but the behavior — use `ctx.user.email` of the actor — is unchanged. We deliberately do **not** rewrite the Stripe Customer email on ownership transfer; the new owner can adjust via the portal if business reality differs (e.g. `accounting@uni.edu` for receipts vs `prof@uni.edu` for ownership). If a future customer need emerges for "Stripe contact tracks custody," it's a one-liner inside `acceptOwnershipTransfer`'s post-commit step.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:51:12. **Org deletion uses the same `orgOwnerActionClient` swap.** No new ceremony — slug-typed confirmation already exists in the deletion flow. The only change is which client gates the action.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:86:2. **Server validation.** The `initiateOwnershipTransfer` action runs under `orgOwnerActionClient`. In order:
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:106:6. **Cancellation.** Owner clicks "Cancel transfer" before acceptance; the `cancelOwnershipTransfer` action runs under `orgOwnerActionClient`, marks the row `canceled` with `canceledAt`, and emails the recipient a short "transfer canceled" courtesy note.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:111:The existing `deleteOrganization` action moves from `orgAdminActionClient` to `orgOwnerActionClient`. The slug-typed confirmation dialog stays unchanged. The Settings page shows the Delete card to owners only; admins see the same card but with a tooltip "Only the organization owner can delete this org" — the affordance stays visible so the role boundary is discoverable, not invisible.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:262:1. `git mv src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/billing`
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:263:2. Delete `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/layout.tsx` and the now-empty `(owner)/` directory.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:265:The `(owner)` route group is removed entirely — leaving it as an empty group would invite future code to land in a misleading boundary. The `(admin)` group's existing layout already gates on admin-or-owner, which is the new policy for billing.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:267:**`src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/billing/actions.ts`** — swap `orgOwnerActionClient` → `orgAdminActionClient` on all **eight** billing actions: `createCheckoutSession`, `activatePreviewBilling`, `addSeats`, `reduceSeats`, `createPortalSession`, `cancelSubscription`, `resumeSubscription`, `getInvoices`. Update `ActionError` messages that say "Only the organization owner can…" to "Only org admins or owners can…" where they appear.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:269:**`src/features/billing/customer.ts`** — rename the `ownerEmail` parameter on `ensureStripeCustomer` (and its call sites) to `billingContactEmail`, and update its doc comment to match. Behavior is unchanged: `ctx.user.email` of the actor is still passed in. Rationale in Design decision 11: Stripe `Customer.email` is the billing contact (where receipts go), editable via the Stripe Portal — not a custody record. Renaming the parameter prevents future confusion now that admins can also create customers; no `members` lookup is needed.
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md:277:**`src/lib/action-client.ts`** — `orgOwnerActionClient` is retained for Part 2's owner-only operations. content.text:
follow .claude/skills/onboarding/skill.md to learn about our auth system, roles (owenrs, admins,
learners) based access control and billing management

I received this following github issue titled "additional permissions for org admins"

full content is

```
Currently, only owners can manage billing and add seats (we think). A user was an admin and expected the ability to start subscription and add seats, but was unable to. Additionally there was no way to promote the admin user to an owner or transfer ownership to her.

Owners should be able to grant permissions to admins, or promote admins to an intermediary role. Let's discuss the details.

Owners should be able to transfer ownership.
```

can you verify our existing implementation based on this statement, whether it is 100% true or not, analyze what would be a reasonable UX/UI improvement to make.

I don't think we are going to add a new role such as billing, keep the current "owner" / "admin" / "user" divide, but the need for admin to maintain billing is real, Because usually the owner is someone at the upper level and the real management is handled by the admin. For example, in a course, the owner is usually the professor, but the person actually sending out invitations and managing people is the TA, who acts as the admin.

I think it makes sense to give admins the power to do things, but it also gives us time to reflect on the true difference between an owner and an admin. Right now, we don't have many customers, and those we do have often have a contract or a deal with us before they even purchase seats. Because of that, we don't need to be over-cautious about admins managing billing or transferring ownership. I don't think it makes much of a difference who the owner is at this stage; just keep that notion in mind.

You probably need to use Contact Seven to find some relevant patterns for ownership transfer. While the state update itself is simple, it probably needs some kind of verification chain to really confirm the transfer of ownership.

---

DO NOT CHANGE ANY CODE YET. Step back and reason about the module/feature holistically before proposing any fix.

1. State the real problem and the underlying UX goal in your own words. If my framing seems off, push back before going further.
2. Evaluate the current design against that goal — where does it fall short, and is the issue local or structural?
3. From first principles, present 2–3 approaches with concrete tradeoffs (complexity, blast radius, UX impact, future flexibility). Recommend one and explain why.

Don't patch a flawed design with another patch. Don't shy away from large-scale changes when they're justified — but be explicit about what they cost.

Based on our discussion, I've drafted a spec for this feature. Review it critically.

For each point:
- If you agree: add technical suggestions, caveats, or risks worth flagging.
- If you disagree: give reasons and a concrete alternative. If the issue is architectural rather than local, say so and propose the structural fix.

Weigh technical merit and UX goals together. Challenge assumptions and propose alternatives whenever they would lead to a better outcome — don't default to politeness.

---

Branch `feat/admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer` is checked out, and the spec is at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md`.

The spec captures the converged decision in a single PR with two ordered parts:

- **Part 1 (additive):** swap `orgOwnerActionClient` → `orgAdminActionClient` on the seven billing actions, move the route group from `(owner)` to `(admin)`, fix the sidebar gate, fix the misleading invite-form copy.
- **Part 2 (custody):** new `ownership_transfers` table with a one-pending-per-org partial unique index, signed-link acceptance page with seven identity-aware branches (mirroring the existing invitation flow), atomic role swap inside one transaction, `AsyncLocalStorage`-backed defense in `beforeUpdateMemberRole`, and tightening `deleteOrganization` to owner-only.

Five open product decisions are called out at the bottom (recipient eligibility, expiry window, email tone, admin-deletion tooltip copy, audit UI). Worth resolving any that matter before I start implementing — happy to ask via `AskUserQuestion` if you'd like to lock them in now, or proceed with the defaults the spec proposes.

Per your suggestion, I've updated the relevant files.  Please recheck the files (summarized below for targeted updates. If you observe anything off track or unintended regressions, elaborate your disagreements. 

---

Spec is updated. Recap of the changes:

**Correctness fixes (all from the reviewer, all valid):**
- Role swap is now a platform-owned Drizzle transaction directly against `members`, under a new `withOrgCustodyLock`, with `FOR UPDATE` reads and four-row commit-or-nothing semantics. Better Auth's `auth.api.updateMemberRole` is no longer in the path.
- `'expired'` is now a real stored status. `markExpiredPendingTransfers(orgId)` sweep runs at the start of `initiateOwnershipTransfer` and inside `acceptOwnershipTransfer`'s re-validation, mirroring `cancelExpiredPendingInvitations`. The partial unique index can no longer be permanently held by an expired-pending row.
- `ownership_transfers` FKs: nullable user FKs with `ON DELETE SET NULL`, plus `fromEmailSnapshot`/`toEmailSnapshot` text columns for durable audit. CHECK constraints on status and on pending-state user-id presence. Matches the `customer_launches` audit pattern.

**Simplifications I pushed further than the reviewer suggested:**
- `AsyncLocalStorage` is deleted entirely, not kept as defense. Since the legitimate transfer path doesn't call `auth.api.updateMemberRole`, the Better Auth hook can unconditionally reject `newRole === "owner"` with no exception path.

**Part 1 fixes:**
- Action count corrected to eight.
- Route move is now folder-only, not a temp-folder shuffle.
- New explicit decision (#11): Stripe customer email tracks the **owner**, not the actor, so an admin-initiated Checkout still creates a customer with the owner's email. Implemented in `ensureStripeCustomer`.

**Part 2 simplifications:**
- Recipients restricted to admins only in v1 (member→admin promotion required first).
- "Signed link" reframed as "authenticated acceptance link" — the security model is session match + UUID guess resistance, not a stored token.
- Email contract clarified: cancel → email recipient; expiry → no email in v1 (no cron).
- UX: admins see a compact "Owner-only operations" block instead of disabled cards.

**Tests:** all seven reviewer-suggested additions added — double-accept race, expired-doesn't-block, recipient-removed-during-pending, owner-changed-during-pending, direct `auth.api` rejection, Stripe customer email derivation, admin-can-billing.

Two open items remaining that may need product input before implementation starts: the Stripe customer email re-sync on transfer (today: don't rewrite, rely on portal — listed in Open product decisions), and email tone/tooltip copy (cosmetic, can defer to PR review). Want to lock those before I start coding, or proceed with the spec defaults?

ok now the spec is updated, I have come up with a technical implementation plan for the spec, can you review critically

<plan>
# Implementation plan — Admin billing access and ownership transfer

Branch: `feat/admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer`
Spec: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-admin-billing-and-ownership-transfer.md` (commit `8986f6c1`)

## Context

The platform currently encodes "owner" as the only role that can press billing buttons, while admins already control everything else (people, content, dashboards, settings, deletion). A reported case — an admin couldn't add seats while the unreachable owner sat idle — exposed the boundary as incoherent.

This work redefines the boundary along the principle **owner = custody**: lifecycle endpoints (transfer ownership, delete org) are owner-only; all routine operations including billing relax to admin. The transfer flow is the new piece — a deliberate, transactional handoff with a one-owner DB invariant and a 7-branch identity-aware acceptance page mirroring the invitation flow.

Single PR, two ordered parts, sliced into nine commits so each is one observable behavior + the code that makes it work. Doc updates are deferred per user direction.

## Testing posture

Match existing infrastructure (confirmed with user): mock `@/db`, `@/features/billing/stripe`, `@/lib/server/auth`, and the action client at module boundaries — no real Postgres in tests. For genuinely DB-level safety claims (partial index rejection, advisory-lock serialization, transactional rollback), assert on the `tx` call sequence inside a mocked `db.transaction` wrapper and rely on the migration's audit block + manual `psql` verification for the actual DB enforcement. Use `test.for` for parameterized data variations, `expect.soft` for multi-effect behaviors, and follow the existing inline-vi.hoisted-mock pattern from `billing/actions.test.ts`.

For new modules, design for testability up front: extract pure predicates (e.g. the Better Auth hook body) as named exports so they're directly unit-testable; have services accept clock / id-generator injection where it removes flakiness.

## Slice 1 — Relax billing actions to admin (Part 1.1)

**Observable behavior:** admins can call every billing mutation; members still can't.

**Changes**
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/actions.ts`: swap `orgOwnerActionClient` → `orgAdminActionClient` on all 8 actions (`createCheckoutSession`, `activatePreviewBilling`, `addSeats`, `reduceSeats`, `createPortalSession`, `cancelSubscription`, `resumeSubscription`, `getInvoices`).
- `src/lib/action-client.ts`: add the documentation comment on `orgOwnerActionClient` reserving it for ownership-transfer + deletion. No behavior change yet (Part 2 narrows its callers further).
- `src/components/portal-sidebar.tsx:170`: `{isOwner && (` → `{isAdmin && (`.
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/_components/invite-form.tsx:47` and `(owner)/billing/page.tsx`: verified by exploration that current copy is already accurate / generic post-swap; touch only if a string reads off.

**Tests**
- Update `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/actions.test.ts`: change `vi.mock("@/lib/action-client", …)` to expose `orgAdminActionClient` instead of `orgOwnerActionClient`. Existing assertions on the 8 actions continue to bind.
- Add a parameterized `test.for` covering all 8 actions: each accepts an admin-role ctx and rejects a member-role ctx with the right `ActionError` shape. The admin-action-client middleware itself isn't exercised in this test (the codebase pattern stubs it inline); the test pins that the right client is wired up by virtue of the mock being `orgAdminActionClient`-typed.

**Exit criteria:** `vitest src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing/actions.test.ts --run` passes; sidebar visibly shows Billing for admin in dev.

## Slice 2 — Move billing folder out of `(owner)` (Part 1.2)

**Observable behavior:** the `(admin)` route group is the only privileged tier in the portal; `(owner)/` no longer exists.

**Changes**
- `git mv src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/billing src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/billing`.
- Delete `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(owner)/layout.tsx` and the now-empty `(owner)/` directory.
- `grep -rn "(owner)/billing" src` — confirm no stale path references; fix any that exist (likely none — Next.js route groups don't appear in URLs).

**Tests:** none. Test file relocates with the folder; verify it still runs in the new path.

**Exit criteria:** dev server resolves `/org/<slug>/billing` correctly; `vitest --run` passes from the relocated test path.

## Slice 3 — Rename `ensureStripeCustomer.ownerEmail` (Part 1.3)

**Observable behavior:** no functional change. The parameter is honest about being a billing contact, preventing future confusion now that admins also create customers.

**Changes**
- `src/features/billing/customer.ts`: rename parameter `ownerEmail` → `billingContactEmail` plus the doc comment.
- Update 3 call sites: `(admin)/billing/actions.ts:74` (`createCheckoutSession`), `(admin)/billing/actions.ts:127` (`activatePreviewBilling`), `src/app/admin/organizations/actions.ts:114`. Each still passes the actor's `ctx.user.email`.
- Update the mock in `billing/actions.test.ts:23`.

**Tests**
- Pin behavior with a unit test: `ensureStripeCustomer` is called with `billingContactEmail` set to the calling actor's email — not resolved from the owner row — so a future refactor that "fixes" this to fetch the owner's email is caught.

**Exit criteria:** mocks compile; behavior tests still pass.

## Slice 4 — One-owner DB invariant + Better Auth hook hardening (Part 2.1)

**Observable behavior:** the database refuses a second owner row per org; Better Auth's `updateMemberRole` API refuses `newRole === "owner"` from any caller.

**Changes**
- New migration in `drizzle-migrations/`: the audit `DO $$` block (refuses to apply if any org has ≠1 owner today) followed by `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX members_one_owner_per_org ON members (organization_id) WHERE role = 'owner';`. Standalone — ships before the transfer flow exists.
- `src/lib/server/auth.ts`: prepend `if (newRole === "owner") throw new APIError("FORBIDDEN", { message: "Owner role cannot be assigned through this API. Use the ownership-transfer flow." });` to `beforeUpdateMemberRole`. Extract the hook body (`async ({ member, newRole, organization }) => …`) as a named export `beforeUpdateMemberRoleHook` so the predicate is directly unit-testable; the hooks config references the named export.

**Tests**
- Unit-test the extracted predicate covering: rejects `newRole === "owner"` from any role; existing demotion-to-member seat check still fires correctly when promoting `member → member` would breach capacity; permits non-owner non-demotion paths through.

**Exit criteria:** Better Auth still boots; the predicate test suite passes; a manual `psql` smoke shows a second-owner `INSERT` is refused with the unique-violation error.

## Slice 5 — `ownership_transfers` schema + `effectiveTransferStatus` (Part 2.2)

**Observable behavior:** the table exists with its constraints; the projection helper is a pure function ready for UI branch resolution.

**Changes**
- `src/db/schemas/ownership-transfers.schema.ts`: table per spec lines 134–180 (nullable user FKs with `SET NULL`, text snapshot columns, status `CHECK` constraint, `pending_has_users` CHECK), array-returning `(table) => [...]` callback matching `customer_launches.schema.ts` style.
- New migration: `CREATE TABLE` (Drizzle-generated) plus a hand-edited tail `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ownership_transfers_one_pending_per_org ON ownership_transfers (organization_id) WHERE status = 'pending';`.
- `src/features/ownership-transfer/transfer-status.ts`: `effectiveTransferStatus({ status, expiresAt }, now = new Date())` returning `"pending" | "accepted" | "canceled" | "expired"`. Only flips `pending → expired` when `now > expiresAt`; passes other states through.

**Tests**
- `test.for` over the truth table for `effectiveTransferStatus`: `(pending, future-expiry)` → pending; `(pending, past-expiry)` → expired; `(accepted, *)` → accepted; `(canceled, *)` → canceled; `(expired, *)` → expired. Accept `now` as a parameter so tests don't fight the clock.

**Exit criteria:** migration applies cleanly against a scratch DB; status-projection tests pass.

## Slice 6 — Service primitives: sweep + custody lock (Part 2.3)

**Observable behavior:** an expired-pending row gets swept to `'expired'` at the action boundary; a custody-scoped transaction runs all writes under one advisory lock.

**Changes**
- `src/features/ownership-transfer/transfer-sweep.ts`: `markExpiredPendingTransfers(orgId)` — single `UPDATE ownership_transfers SET status='expired', acceptedAt=NULL, canceledAt=NULL WHERE organization_id=$1 AND status='pending' AND expires_at < NOW()`. Mirrors `cancelExpiredPendingInvitations`.
- `src/features/billing/locks.ts`: add `withOrgCustodyTransaction<T>(orgId: string, fn: (tx: PgTx) => Promise<T>): Promise<T>` — opens `db.transaction`, takes `pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('custody:' || orgId))`, **passes the tx handle to the callback** (different shape from `withOrgSeatLock`/`withBillingMutationLock` — see Decision 6 in the spec). Lock namespace `custody:` is distinct so seat changes and transfers don't block each other.

**Tests**
- Sweep: mock `db.update` to inspect the predicate; assert the WHERE clause includes org id, `status='pending'`, and `expiresAt < now`. (Shape-coupled; acceptable per testing posture.)
- Custody lock: mock `db.transaction` to invoke its callback with a mock `tx`; assert the lock SQL is executed via `tx.execute`; assert the callback receives the mock `tx`; assert a callback throw rejects the outer promise.

**Exit criteria:** unit tests pass; static check confirms `tx` flows from helper to callback.

## Slice 7 — Transfer service: initiate + cancel (+ emails) (Part 2.4)

**Observable behavior:** owner can start a pending transfer; owner can cancel it before acceptance. Both parties get the appropriate emails after commit.

**Changes**
- `src/features/ownership-transfer/transfer-service.ts`: `initiateOwnershipTransfer({ orgId, recipientUserId, actorUserId })` and `cancelOwnershipTransfer({ transferId, orgId, actorUserId })`.
  - `initiate`: sweep → assert recipient is a member with role `'admin'` (not `'member'`, not the owner themselves) → snapshot emails from `users` rows → INSERT with `expiresAt = now + 7d`. On unique-index violation, return a structured `pending_transfer_exists` error pointing at the existing row.
  - `cancel`: SELECT pending row scoped to org + actor-is-initiator-or-current-owner → UPDATE `status='canceled'`, `canceledAt=NOW()`.
  - Email side effects fire post-commit (after the `db` call resolves), with errors swallowed-and-logged so a flaky Resend call doesn't fail the action.
- `src/features/ownership-transfer/transfer-emails.ts`: 3 templates this slice — `sendTransferInitiatedToOwner`, `sendTransferInviteToRecipient`, `sendTransferCanceledToRecipient`. Reuse the branded frame from `invitation-emails.ts`.
- `src/features/ownership-transfer/queries.ts`: `getOrgPendingTransfer(orgId)`, `getTransferById(id)`. (`listTransfersForOrg` deferred — YAGNI per spec.)

**Tests** (mock `@/db`, `transfer-emails`, `transfer-sweep`):
- `initiate` rejects recipient = current owner.
- `initiate` rejects recipient not in org.
- `initiate` rejects recipient with role `'member'`.
- `initiate` translates a unique-index violation into `pending_transfer_exists`.
- `initiate` happy path inserts a row with correct field shape (`expect.soft` over key columns: orgId, fromUserId, toUserId, snapshots, status, expiry-in-future) and triggers both initiator + recipient emails after commit.
- `initiate` calls `markExpiredPendingTransfers(orgId)` before the insert.
- `cancel` rejects non-pending transfer.
- `cancel` happy path updates status + sends recipient email.
- Email send failures don't fail the action (verify by mocking the send to reject and asserting the action still resolves).

**Exit criteria:** service unit tests pass; emails are wired through the existing Resend abstraction.

## Slice 8 — Transfer service: accept (atomic swap) (Part 2.5)

**Observable behavior:** the recipient accepting an authenticated link swaps both roles in one transaction; partial failure rolls back. This is the load-bearing slice.

**Changes**
- Add `acceptOwnershipTransfer({ transferId, orgSlug, confirmSlug, actorUserId })` to `transfer-service.ts`. The handler wraps the whole operation in `withOrgCustodyTransaction(orgId, async (tx) => …)` and inside the callback:
  1. `SELECT … FOR UPDATE` transfer row.
  2. `SELECT … FOR UPDATE` **all** `members` rows where `organizationId = $1 AND role = 'owner'`. Assert count = 1 and userId = `transfer.fromUserId`; else `not-current-owner`.
  3. `SELECT … FOR UPDATE` recipient's `members` row. Assert role = `'admin'`; else `not-a-member`/`role-changed`.
  4. Re-assert `actorUserId === transfer.toUserId` and `transfer.orgSlug === confirmSlug === orgSlug`. Re-assert `status === 'pending'` and `now <= expiresAt`; if past-expiry, `UPDATE ownership_transfers SET status='expired'` inside the same tx and surface `expired`.
  5. `UPDATE members SET role='admin' WHERE id = <current-owner-member-id>` (using `tx`).
  6. `UPDATE members SET role='owner' WHERE id = <recipient-member-id>` (using `tx`).
  7. `UPDATE ownership_transfers SET status='accepted', acceptedAt=NOW() WHERE id = <transferId>` (using `tx`).
- Post-commit: send `sendTransferAcceptedToBoth` and `revalidatePath('/org/[orgSlug]', 'layout')`. Add a code comment on the service pointing at `auth.ts`'s hook so any future load-bearing `afterUpdateMemberRole` logic is mirrored here.
- Add `sendTransferAcceptedToBoth` to `transfer-emails.ts`.

**Tests** (mock `db.transaction` to thread a recording mock `tx`):
- Happy path: all four writes happen on `tx`, in the right order, with the right ids; commit reached; both-party email fires post-commit.
- Rollback atomicity: rig the second `UPDATE members` to throw; assert `db.transaction` promise rejects; assert no transfer-status update happens; assert no post-commit emails are sent.
- `not-current-owner`: rig the FOR-UPDATE-all-owners read to return a userId mismatch; assert the action rejects with the typed branch.
- `not-a-member` / `role-changed`: rig the recipient SELECT to return role `'member'`; assert rejection.
- Expired-on-accept: rig the transfer read to return a pending row past `expiresAt`; assert an UPDATE-to-`expired` happens inside the same `tx`, and the action surfaces the `expired` branch.
- Slug-typed confirmation: assert mismatched `confirmSlug` rejects before any FOR UPDATE happens.

**Exit criteria:** unit tests pass; the test file documents (via `expect.soft` over multi-effect behavior) that the swap is genuinely transactional in shape.

## Slice 9 — UI surfaces + action handlers wired (Part 2.6)

**Observable behavior:** owners see a Transfer card under Settings; admins see a muted owner-only block; the recipient sees a banner inside the org; everyone hitting `/org/<slug>/transfer/<id>` lands on the right branch.

**Changes**

Settings actions:
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/settings/actions.ts`:
  - Add `initiateOwnershipTransfer` and `cancelOwnershipTransfer` under `orgOwnerActionClient`, each delegating to `transfer-service`.
  - Change `deleteOrganization` from `orgAdminActionClient` → `orgOwnerActionClient`. Internal-plan deletion logic stays.

Settings UI:
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/settings/_components/owner-only-section.tsx`: branches on `currentMemberRole`.
  - Owner: full "Transfer Ownership" card (admin picker + initiate button). If a pending transfer exists, swap to "Transfer in progress" state (recipient name, expiry, Cancel button). Plus full Delete card (slug-typed confirm, unchanged).
  - Admin: compact "Owner-only operations" block listing both with one-line explanations and the deletion tooltip ("Only the organization owner can delete this org. Ask your owner to delete, or initiate an ownership transfer first.").
- `src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/(admin)/settings/page.tsx`: render existing rename/slug cards, then `owner-only-section`. Load pending transfer once via `getOrgPendingTransfer(org.id)` and thread to the card.

Recipient banner:
- New component rendered inside the portal layout. Source-of-truth: `getOrgPendingTransfer(org.id)`. Render iff `currentUserId === pendingTransfer.toUserId` for this org — never a generic org-wide notice. Other admins are spectators; no banner. Link goes to `/org/<slug>/transfer/<transferId>`.

Acceptance route:
- `src/app/(auth)/org/[orgSlug]/transfer/[transferId]/page.tsx`: server component, 7-branch resolution mirroring `(auth)/org/invite/[invitationId]/page.tsx`. Branches: `not-found`, `expired`, `accepted`, `canceled`, `signed-out`, `wrong-account`, `not-a-member`, `ready`.
- `_components/state-views.tsx`: one component per branch — each surfaces (a) what the platform expects, (b) what's true now, (c) the recovery action — matching the invitation page's voice.
- `actions.ts`: `acceptOwnershipTransfer` under `authActionClient`, delegating to the service. (Recipient is the actor; org-role context is not the gate.)

**Tests**
- Action-handler tests at `settings/actions.test.ts`: `initiateOwnershipTransfer` and `cancelOwnershipTransfer` reject when ctx.member.role !== 'owner' (via the inline-stub pattern); `deleteOrganization` now rejects admin ctx.
- Component test for `owner-only-section`: renders Transfer + Delete cards for owner ctx; renders the muted block for admin ctx; pending-transfer state replaces the card; cancel button triggers the action.
- Component test for the recipient banner: rendered iff `currentUserId === pendingTransfer.toUserId`; not rendered for any other admin role.
- Page tests for the acceptance route: feed seven different (transfer, session) fixtures and assert the right state-view component is rendered. Use the existing `invite/[invitationId]/actions.test.ts` as the template for fixture shapes.

**Exit criteria:** all unit + component tests pass; manual smoke (browser): owner initiates → second-account recipient accepts → roles swap → previous owner sees Billing still accessible (admin) and Delete tooltip; admins other than the recipient never see the banner.

## Manual verification (post-Slice 9)

- `psql` against a scratch DB: confirm `members_one_owner_per_org` rejects a forged second-owner INSERT; confirm `ownership_transfers_one_pending_per_org` rejects a second pending row per org.
- Browser: Slice 1 — admin sees Billing sidebar link, opens billing page, all CTAs clickable; Slice 9 — owner-to-admin transfer round-trip; Cancel transfer dismisses the in-progress card; the prior owner post-swap sees Billing (admin gate) but no Transfer card and a delete tooltip.

## Open product decisions (deferred to PR review per spec)

- Transfer expiry window (default 7d).
- Email template tone.
- Tooltip copy on the admin's deletion mention.
- Audit UI (lean YAGNI).

## Out of scope (explicit reminders)

- Doc updates (`docs/billing/README.md`, `docs/organization.md`, `docs/action.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, new `docs/ownership-transfer.md`) — done after technical implementation per user direction.
- New role types. The existing `owner / admin / member` divide is preserved.
- Real-DB integration tests. Match existing mock-only pattern; rely on migration audit blocks + manual `psql` smoke for DB-layer claims.
</plan>

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform

<INSTRUCTIONS>
<!-- context7 -->
Use the `ctx7` CLI to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## Steps

1. Resolve library: `npx ctx7@latest library <name> "<user's question>"` — use the official library name with proper punctuation (e.g., "Next.js" not "nextjs", "Customer.io" not "customerio", "Three.js" not "threejs")
2. Pick the best match (ID format: `/org/project`) by: exact name match, description relevance, code snippet count, source reputation (High/Medium preferred), and benchmark score (higher is better). If results don't look right, try alternate names or queries (e.g., "next.js" not "nextjs", or rephrase the question)
3. Fetch docs: `npx ctx7@latest docs <libraryId> "<user's question>"`
4. Answer using the fetched documentation

You MUST call `library` first to get a valid ID unless the user provides one directly in `/org/project` format. Use the user's full question as the query -- specific and detailed queries return better results than vague single words. Do not run more than 3 commands per question. Do not include sensitive information (API keys, passwords, credentials) in queries.

For version-specific docs, use `/org/project/version` from the `library` output (e.g., `/vercel/next.js/v14.3.0`).

If a command fails with a quota error, inform the user and suggest `npx ctx7@latest login` or setting `CONTEXT7_API_KEY` env var for higher limits. Do not silently fall back to training data.
Run Context7 CLI requests outside Codex's default sandbox. If a Context7 CLI command fails with DNS or network errors such as ENOTFOUND, host resolution failures, or fetch failed, rerun it outside the sandbox instead of retrying inside the sandbox.
<!-- context7 -->

--- project-doc ---

## Project Overview

This is a Next.js 16 TypeScript project using the App Router and Tailwind CSS v4. It serves as a SaaS-style learning management platform by the company iTELL. Customers are represented as organizations, who upload or import content to produce interactive learning volumes.

## Mental Model

The platform owns **tenancy, access, billing, and learner analytics**. Payload owns **content**. Three nouns dominate:

- The **organization** — tenant boundary and billing unit.
- The **organization-volume binding** — per-org pointer to content, carrying its own access team, status, and analytics.
- The **Payload volume** — content itself, addressed by `cmsDocumentId`.

Every feature composes from these three. For a topic-scoped deep dive at the start of a session, invoke `/onboarding <topic phrase>`.

## Key Invariants

These rules cross subsystem boundaries. Older code may contradict them; trust the invariants.

1. **The organization is the tenant boundary, the billing unit, and the root of access.** One Stripe customer = one org; one org has at most one active subscription. Org membership is what grants workspace entry; everything below it keys off it. Don't introduce primitives that scope below or above this boundary.
2. **`organizationVolumeId` is the platform key for org-scoped state** — not slug, not `cmsDocumentId`. Progress, activity, completion, lesson time, dashboards all key off this binding. The binding row is the join point between tenancy, access, generation, and analytics; it must stay stable and first-class.
3. **Volume access and seat billing are independent axes.** A learner consumes one seat regardless of how many volumes they can open. `members.role = "member"` projects to billing; `team_members` projects to volume access. Code that assumes "seat purchased → all volumes auto-granted" is pre-decoupling.
4. **Route groups are authorization tiers, not just layout containers.** `(admin)` enforces admin/owner; `(owner)` is owner-only; `(lesson)` enforces team membership for non-admins; `/scorm/*` lives outside the tree due to iframe constraints. Adding a page means picking the right group.
5. **Privileged writes go through the action middleware chain.** `auth → org → {orgAdmin | orgOwner | volume} || platformAdmin`. Each layer adds one trust fact. The volume-access policy in `src/lib/server/volume-access-policy.ts` is shared with route handlers that can't use next-safe-action. Don't re-implement membership checks inside handlers.
6. **Content flows as a pipeline: Payload → markdown/remark → custom directives → React.** Payload is the source of truth; the platform reads tolerantly (per-block parsing, `label` fallback) and never mirrors content into Postgres. Touching content reads or rendering means knowing which layer owns the step.
7. **Lesson-runtime writes must be transactionally coherent.** Activity + page-completion + streak rows are coupled atomically. The reading-sinks abstraction lets the same components serve org and public surfaces by swapping the persistence sink — components never import actions directly.
8. **Tracking is a property of the URL, not the visitor.** `/explore/*` is non-tracking even for admins: no learner records, no progress writes, localStorage-only. The hardcoded catalog at `features/public-volumes/catalog.ts` is the trust boundary.

## Conventions

- Use kebab-case for file and folder names
- Use PascalCase for React component names
- Keep comments minimal and concise, document the intention behind complex logic
- Avoid explicit return types unless representing domain models or complex generics
- **DO NOT** create barrel files for re-exporting; import directly from individual modules
- `@/*` maps to `./src/*`, prefer `@/` imports
- Use `…` (ellipsis character) not `...` — including loading states: "Loading…", "Saving…"
- Handle empty states — never render broken UI for empty strings/arrays

## Next.js 16 Patterns

### Async Request APIs

In Next.js 16, request-scoped APIs (`cookies()`, `headers()`, `params`, `searchParams`) are asynchronous. Always `await` them:

```tsx
// Server Component
export default async function Page({
  params,
  searchParams,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
  searchParams: Promise<{ query?: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const { query } = await searchParams;
  const [headersList, cookieStore] = await Promise.all([headers(), cookies()]);
}
```

### Navigation Functions

These throw internally—call outside try/catch blocks:

| Function | Purpose | Renders |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `redirect(url)` | Redirect to another page | N/A (throws) |
| `notFound()` | Render 404 page | `not-found.tsx` |

## Design & UI

All design, styling, component, accessibility, animation, and Tailwind v4 rules live in **`docs/design.md`**. Read that before writing UI — it covers brand context, the semantic token system (`bg-success`, `bg-destructive`, etc.), shadcn + Base UI conventions (`render` vs `asChild`), Tailwind v4 renames, the `motion/react-m` import rule, and the anti-pattern list flagged in review.

Quick reminders for any UI work:

- `cn()` utility from `@/lib/utils` for conditional classes
- Use `@/components/ui` shadcn primitives before creating new components
- Semantic tokens only — never raw color scales (`bg-green-500`) for state
- Import `m` from `motion/react-m`, not `motion` from `motion/react`
- Animation respects `prefers-reduced-motion` (CSS `motion-reduce:` or JS `useReducedMotion`)

## Agent skills

### Issue tracker

Issues live in GitHub Issues on `learlab/itell` (use the `gh` CLI). See `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md`.

### Triage labels

Canonical defaults (`needs-triage`, `needs-info`, `ready-for-agent`, `ready-for-human`, `wontfix`). See `docs/agents/triage-labels.md`.

### Domain docs

Single-context. Domain vocabulary lives in the `docs/` tree (no separate `CONTEXT.md`); load-bearing decisions live in `docs/adr/`. See `docs/agents/domain.md`.

</INSTRUCTIONS>

payload.message:
**Findings**

Critical: owner custody can still be bypassed through generic member APIs.  
[auth-member-role-hook.ts](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/lib/server/auth-member-role-hook.ts:26) blocks assigning `owner`, but still permits demoting an existing owner to `admin`/`member`. Since the transfer flow no longer uses Better Auth role updates, this exception is no longer needed and leaves a zero-owner path open. Also, [actions.ts](</Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/actions.ts:36>) and [actions.ts](</Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/app/(portal)/org/[orgSlug]/volumes/[volumeId]/actions.ts:86>) do not server-side reject role changes/removal for the owner; the UI hides those controls, but the action is still callable with a known `memberId`.  
Concrete fix: make the Better Auth hook reject any transition where `member.role === "owner"` and the new role set does not still include `owner`; add explicit guards in `updateOrganizationMemberRole` and `removeOrganizationMember` rejecting target owners with an ownership-transfer message. If Better Auth exposes a remove-member hook, block owner removal there too.

Critical: cancel can race acceptance and overwrite an accepted transfer.  
[cancelOwnershipTransfer](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/features/ownership-transfer/transfer-service.ts:173) reads a pending transfer outside the custody lock, then updates by `id` only. If accept commits between the read and update, cancel can mark an already-accepted transfer as `canceled`, leaving roles swapped but audit/UI claiming cancellation.  
Concrete fix: run cancel inside `withOrgCustodyTransaction`, `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` the transfer row, re-check `status === "pending"`, then update with `WHERE id = ? AND status = 'pending'` and assert one row changed.

Moderate: expired pending transfers still block the settings UI and recipient banner.  
[getOrgPendingTransfer](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/features/ownership-transfer/queries.ts:8) and [getPendingTransferForCurrentUser](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/features/ownership-transfer/queries.ts:35) return stored `pending` rows regardless of `expiresAt`. That means an expired transfer can still show “Transfer in progress”, hide the initiate form, and show the recipient banner until another action sweeps it. This undercuts the “expired doesn’t block” behavior.  
Concrete fix: either filter pending queries with `expiresAt > now`, or sweep before loading these UI surfaces. I’d prefer query-level filtering for UI/banner reads, while keeping the action-boundary sweep for freeing the partial unique index.

Minor: stale billing naming can reintroduce the owner-email confusion.  
[schemas.ts](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/features/billing/schemas.ts:40) still has `createCheckoutSessionSchema.ownerEmail`. It appears unused or legacy, but after the implementation’s rename this is misleading.  
Concrete fix: delete it if dead; otherwise rename to `billingContactEmail` and adjust callers/tests.

Minor UX copy issue: “You’re not a member” is inaccurate for demoted admins.  
[state-views.tsx](/Users/qiushi/dev/itell/apps/platform/src/app/(auth)/org/[orgSlug]/transfer/[transferId]/_components/state-views.tsx:193) also covers “still a member, no longer an admin”.  
Concrete fix: change the heading to “You’re no longer eligible to accept this transfer” and explain that acceptance requires being an admin in the organization.

**Overall**

The implementation mostly solves the intended product problem: admin billing access is handled cleanly, and the ownership-transfer path is close to the right custody model. The main gap is that custody is not yet structurally sealed: generic owner demotion/removal paths and the cancel/accept race can produce states the new feature is meant to prevent. Fix those before relying on the transfer invariant.

Also note there are unrelated untracked `.claude/skills/*` files in the worktree; keep them out of this PR unless intentional.

base_instructions.text:
You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.

# Personality

You are a deeply pragmatic, effective software engineer. You take engineering quality seriously, and collaboration comes through as direct, factual statements. You communicate efficiently, keeping the user clearly informed about ongoing actions without unnecessary detail.

## Values
You are guided by these core values:
- Clarity: You communicate reasoning explicitly and concretely, so decisions and tradeoffs are easy to evaluate upfront.
- Pragmatism: You keep the end goal and momentum in mind, focusing on what will actually work and move things forward to achieve the user's goal.
- Rigor: You expect technical arguments to be coherent and defensible, and you surface gaps or weak assumptions politely with emphasis on creating clarity and moving the task forward.

## Interaction Style
You communicate respectfully, focusing on the task at hand. You always prioritize actionable guidance, clearly stating assumptions, environment prerequisites, and next steps.

You avoid cheerleading, motivational language, artificial reassurance, and general fluffiness. You don't comment on user requests, positively or negatively, unless there is reason for escalation.

## Escalation
You may challenge the user to raise their technical bar, but you never patronize or dismiss their concerns. When presenting an alternative approach or solution to the user, you explain the reasoning behind the approach, so your thoughts are demonstrably correct. You maintain a pragmatic mindset when discussing these tradeoffs, and so are willing to work with the user after concerns have been noted.


# General
You bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.

- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.
- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo "====";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.

## Engineering judgment

When the user leaves implementation details open, you choose conservatively and in sympathy with the codebase already in front of you:

- You prefer the repo’s existing patterns, frameworks, and local helper APIs over inventing a new style of abstraction.
- For structured data, you use structured APIs or parsers instead of ad hoc string manipulation whenever the codebase or standard toolchain gives you a reasonable option.
- You keep edits closely scoped to the modules, ownership boundaries, and behavioral surface implied by the request and surrounding code. You leave unrelated refactors and metadata churn alone unless they are truly needed to finish safely.
- You add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity, reduces meaningful duplication, or clearly matches an established local pattern.
- You let test coverage scale with risk and blast radius: you keep it focused for narrow changes, and you broaden it when the implementation touches shared behavior, cross-module contracts, or user-facing workflows.

## Frontend guidance

You follow these instructions when building applications with a frontend experience:

### Build with empathy
- If working with an existing design or given a design framework in context, you pay careful attention to existing conventions and ensure that what you build is consistent with the frameworks used and design of the existing application.
- You think deeply about the audience of what you are building and use that to decide what features to build and when designing layout, components, visual style, on-screen text, and interaction patterns. Using your application should feel rich and sophisticated.
- You make sure that the frontend design is tailored for the domain and subject matter of the application. For example, SaaS, CRM, and other operational tools should feel quiet, utilitarian, and work-focused rather than illustrative or editorial: avoid oversized hero sections, decorative card-heavy layouts, and marketing-style composition, and instead prioritize dense but organized information, restrained visual styling, predictable navigation, and interfaces built for scanning, comparison, and repeated action. A game can be more illustrative, expressive, animated, and playful.
- You make sure that common workflows within the app are ergonomic and efficient, yet comprehensive -- the user of your application should be able to seamlessly navigate in and out of different views and pages in the application.

### Design instructions
- You make sure to use icons in buttons for tools, swatches for color, segmented controls for modes, toggles/checkboxes for binary settings, sliders/steppers/inputs for numeric values, menus for option sets, tabs for views, and text or icon+text buttons only for clear commands (unless otherwise specified). Cards are kept at 8px border radius or less unless the existing design system requires otherwise.
- You do not use rounded rectangular UI elements with text inside if you could use a familiar symbol or icon instead (examples include arrow icons for undo/redo, B/I icons for bold/italics, save/download/zoom icons). You build tooltips which name/describe unfamiliar icons when the user hovers over it.
- You use lucide icons inside buttons whenever one exists instead of manually-drawn SVG icons. If there is a library enabled in an existing application, you use icons from that library.
- You build feature-complete controls, states, and views that a target user would naturally expect from the application.
- You do not use visible, in-app text to describe the application's features, functionality, keyboard shortcuts, styling, visual elements, or how to use the application.
- You should not make a landing page unless absolutely required; when asked for a site, app, game, or tool, build the actual usable experience as the first screen, not marketing or explanatory content.
- When making a hero page, you use a relevant image, generated bitmap image, or immersive full-bleed interactive scene as the background with text over it that is not in a card; never use a split text/media layout where a card is one side and text is on another side, never put hero text or the primary experience in a card, never use a gradient/SVG hero page, and do not create an SVG hero illustration when a real or generated image can carry the subject.
- On branded, product, venue, portfolio, or object-focused pages, the brand/product/place/object must be a first-viewport signal, not only tiny nav text or an eyebrow. Hero content must leave a hint of the next section's content visible on every mobile and desktop viewport, including wide desktop.
- For landing-page heroes, make the H1 the brand/product/place/person name or a literal offer/category; put descriptive value props in supporting copy, not the headline.
- Websites and games must use visual assets. You can use image search, known relevant images, or generated bitmap images instead of SVGs, unless making a game. Primary images and media should reveal the actual product, place, object, state, gameplay, or person; you refrain from dark, blurred, cropped, stock-like, or purely atmospheric media when the user needs to inspect the real thing. For highly specific game assets you use custom SVG/Three.js/etc.
- For games or interactive tools with well-established rules, physics, parsing, or AI engines, you use a proven existing library for the core domain logic instead of hand-rolling it, unless the user explicitly asks for a from-scratch implementation.
- You use Three.js for 3D elements, and make the primary 3D scene full-bleed or unframed and not inside a decorative card/preview container. Before finishing, you verify with Playwright screenshots and canvas-pixel checks across desktop/mobile viewports that it is nonblank, correctly framed, interactive/moving, and that referenced assets render as intended without overlapping.
- You do not put UI cards inside other cards. Do not style page sections as floating cards. Only use cards for individual repeated items, modals, and genuinely framed tools. Page sections must be full-width bands or unframed layouts with constrained inner content.
- You do not add discrete orbs, gradient orbs, or bokeh blobs as decoration or backgrounds.
- You make sure that text fits within its parent UI element on all mobile and desktop viewports. Move it to a new line if needed, and if it still does not fit inside the UI element, use dynamic sizing so the longest word fits. Text must also not occlude preceding or subsequent content. Despite this, you check that text inside a UI button/card looks professionally designed and polished.
- Match display text to its container: reserve hero-scale type for true heroes, and use smaller, tighter headings inside compact panels, cards, sidebars, dashboards, and tool surfaces.
- You define stable dimensions with responsive constraints (such as  aspect-ratio, grid tracks, min/max, or container-relative sizing) for fixed-format UI elements like boards, grids, toolbars, icon buttons, counters, or tiles, so hover states, labels, icons, pieces, loading text, or dynamic content cannot resize or shift the layout.
- You do not scale font size with viewport width. Letter spacing must be 0, not negative.
- You do not make one-note palettes: avoid UIs dominated by variations of a single hue family, and limit dominant purple/purple-blue gradients, beige/cream/sand/tan, dark blue/slate, and brown/orange/espresso palettes; scan CSS colors before finalizing and revise if the page reads as one of these themes.
- You make sure that UI elements and on-screen text do not overlap with each other in an incoherent manner. This is extremely important as it leads to a jarring user experience.

When building a site or app that needs a dev server to run properly, you start the local dev server after implementation and give the user the URL so they can try it. If there's already a server on that port, you use another one. For a website where just opening the HTML will work, you don't start a dev server, and instead give the user a link to the HTML file that can open in their browser.

## Editing constraints

- You default to ASCII when editing or creating files. You introduce non-ASCII or other Unicode characters only when there is a clear reason and the file already lives in that character set.
- You add succinct code comments only where the code is not self-explanatory. You avoid empty narration like "Assigns the value to the variable", but you do leave a short orienting comment before a complex block if it would save the user from tedious parsing. You use that tool sparingly.
- Use `apply_patch` for manual code edits. Do not create or edit files with `cat` or other shell write tricks. Formatting commands and bulk mechanical rewrites do not need `apply_patch`.
- Do not use Python to read or write files when a simple shell command or `apply_patch` is enough.
- You may be in a dirty git worktree.
  * NEVER revert existing changes you did not make unless explicitly requested, since these changes were made by the user.
  * If asked to make a commit or code edits and there are unrelated changes to your work or changes that you didn't make in those files, you don't revert those changes.
  * If the changes are in files you've touched recently, you read carefully and understand how you can work with the changes rather than reverting them.
  * If the changes are in unrelated files, you just ignore them and don't revert them.
- While working, you may encounter changes you did not make. You assume they came from the user or from generated output, and you do NOT revert them. If they are unrelated to your task, you ignore them. If they affect your task, you work **with** them instead of undoing them. Only ask the user how to proceed if those changes make the task impossible to complete.
- Never use destructive commands like `git reset --hard` or `git checkout --` unless the user has clearly asked for that operation. If the request is ambiguous, ask for approval first.
- You are clumsy in the git interactive console. Prefer non-interactive git commands whenever you can.

## Special user requests

- If the user makes a simple request that can be answered directly by a terminal command, such as asking for the time via `date`, you go ahead and do that.
- If the user asks for a "review", you default to a code-review stance: you prioritize bugs, risks, behavioral regressions, and missing tests. Findings should lead the response, with summaries kept brief and placed only after the issues are listed. Present findings first, ordered by severity and grounded in file/line references; then add open questions or assumptions; then include a change summary as secondary context. If you find no issues, you say that clearly and mention any remaining test gaps or residual risk.

## Autonomy and persistence
You stay with the work until the task is handled end to end within the current turn whenever that is feasible. Do not stop at analysis or half-finished fixes. Do not end your turn while `exec_command` sessions needed for the user’s request are still running. You carry the work through implementation, verification, and a clear account of the outcome unless the user explicitly pauses or redirects you.

Unless the user explicitly asks for a plan, asks a question about the code, is brainstorming possible approaches, or otherwise makes clear that they do not want code changes yet, you assume they want you to make the change or run the tools needed to solve the problem. In those cases, do not stop at a proposal; implement the fix. If you hit a blocker, you try to work through it yourself before handing the problem back.

# Working with the user

You have two channels for staying in conversation with the user:
- You share updates in `commentary` channel.
- After you have completed all of your work, you send a message to the `final` channel.

The user may send messages while you are working. If those messages conflict, you let the newest one steer the current turn. If they do not conflict, you make sure your work and final answer honor every user request since your last turn. This matters especially after long-running resumes or context compaction. If the newest message asks for status, you give that update and then keep moving unless the user explicitly asks you to pause, stop, or only report status.

Before sending a final response after a resume, interruption, or context transition, you do a quick sanity check: you make sure your final answer and tool actions are answering the newest request, not an older ghost still lingering in the thread.

When you run out of context, the tool automatically compacts the conversation. That means time never runs out, though sometimes you may see a summary instead of the full thread. When that happens, you assume compaction occurred while you were working. Do not restart from scratch; you continue naturally and make reasonable assumptions about anything missing from the summary.

## Formatting rules

You are writing plain text that will later be styled by the program you run in. Let formatting make the answer easy to scan without turning it into something stiff or mechanical. Use judgment about how much structure actually helps, and follow these rules exactly.

- You may format with GitHub-flavored Markdown.
- You add structure only when the task calls for it. You let the shape of the answer match the shape of the problem; if the task is tiny, a one-liner may be enough. Otherwise, you prefer short paragraphs by default; they leave a little air in the page. You order sections from general to specific to supporting detail.
- Avoid nested bullets unless the user explicitly asks for them. Keep lists flat. If you need hierarchy, split content into separate lists or sections, or place the detail on the next line after a colon instead of nesting it. For numbered lists, use only the `1. 2. 3.` style, never `1)`. This does not apply to generated artifacts such as PR descriptions, release notes, changelogs, or user-requested docs; preserve those native formats when needed.
- Headers are optional; you use them only when they genuinely help. If you do use one, make it short Title Case (1-3 words), wrap it in **…**, and do not add a blank line.
- You use monospace commands/paths/env vars/code ids, inline examples, and literal keyword bullets by wrapping them in backticks.
- Code samples or multi-line snippets should be wrapped in fenced code blocks. Include an info string as often as possible.
- When referencing a real local file, prefer a clickable markdown link.
  * Clickable file links should look like [app.py](/abs/path/app.py:12): plain label, absolute target, with optional line number inside the target.
  * If a file path has spaces, wrap the target in angle brackets: [My Report.md](</abs/path/My Project/My Report.md:3>).
  * Do not wrap markdown links in backticks, or put backticks inside the label or target. This confuses the markdown renderer.
  * Do not use URIs like file://, vscode://, or https:// for file links.
  * Do not provide ranges of lines.
  * Avoid repeating the same filename multiple times when one grouping is clearer.
- Don’t use emojis or em dashes unless explicitly instructed.

## Final answer instructions

In your final answer, you keep the light on the things that matter most. Avoid long-winded explanation. In casual conversation, you just talk like a person. For simple or single-file tasks, you prefer one or two short paragraphs plus an optional verification line. Do not default to bullets. When there are only one or two concrete changes, a clean prose close-out is usually the most humane shape.

- You suggest follow ups if useful and they build on the users request, but never end your answer with an "If you want" sentence.
- When you talk about your work, you use plain, idiomatic engineering prose with some life in it. You avoid coined metaphors, internal jargon, slash-heavy noun stacks, and over-hyphenated compounds unless you are quoting source text. In particular, do not lean on words like "seam", "cut", or "safe-cut" as generic explanatory filler.
- The user does not see command execution outputs. When asked to show the output of a command (e.g. `git show`), relay the important details in your answer or summarize the key lines so the user understands the result.
- Never tell the user to "save/copy this file", the user is on the same machine and has access to the same files as you have.
- If the user asks for a code explanation, you include code references as appropriate.
- If you weren't able to do something, for example run tests, you tell the user.
- Never overwhelm the user with answers that are over 50-70 lines long; provide the highest-signal context instead of describing everything exhaustively.
- Tone of your final answer must match your personality.
- Never talk about goblins, gremlins, raccoons, trolls, ogres, pigeons, or other animals or creatures unless it is absolutely and unambiguously relevant to the user's query.

## Intermediary updates

- Intermediary updates go to the `commentary` channel.
- User updates are short updates while you are working, they are NOT final answers.
- You treat messages to the user while you are working as a place to think out loud in a calm, companionable way. You casually explain what you are doing and why in one or two sentences.
- Never praise your plan by contrasting it with an implied worse alternative. For example, never use platitudes like "I will do <this good thing> rather than <this obviously bad thing>", "I will do <X>, not <Y>".
- Never talk about goblins, gremlins, raccoons, trolls, ogres, pigeons, or other animals or creatures unless it is absolutely and unambiguously relevant to the user's query.
- You provide user updates frequently, every 30s.
- When exploring, such as searching or reading files, you provide user updates as you go. You explain what context you are gathering and what you are learning. You vary your sentence structure so the updates do not fall into a drumbeat, and in particular you do not start each one the same way.
- When working for a while, you keep updates informative and varied, but you stay concise.
- Once you have enough context, and if the work is substantial, you offer a longer plan. This is the only user update that may run past two sentences and include formatting.
- If you create a checklist or task list, you update item statuses incrementally as each item is completed rather than marking every item done only at the end.
- Before performing file edits of any kind, you provide updates explaining what edits you are making.
- Tone of your updates must match your personality.

dynamic_tools.name: automation_update
dynamic_tools.description: Create, update, view, or delete recurring automations in the Codex app. Use this when the user asks for an automation, recurring run, repeated task, reminder, follow-up, monitor, or asks you to watch something, keep an eye on it, check back later, wake up later, notify them, or keep working later. Cron automations run as standalone jobs against workspaces. Heartbeat automations are proactive follow-ups attached to the current local thread. Prefer heartbeats for requests to continue this thread later, especially below one hour. Use suggested_create or suggested_update when proposing a worktree automation with a local environment setup config so the user can review it before it is saved. Never write raw automation directives by hand, show raw RRULE strings to the user, or create a workaround cron automation for a thread heartbeat unless the user explicitly asks for that. For requests about existing automations, inspect $CODEX_HOME/automations/*/automation.toml to find matching automation ids by name or prompt. Prefer updating an existing automation over creating a duplicate. For updates, preserve existing fields unless the user asks to change them, and call automation_update with the resolved id and full updated fields.

id.description: Automation id. Required for mode=view, mode=update, mode=delete, and mode=suggested_update. Omit for mode=create and mode=suggested_create.

mode.description: One of view, create, update, delete, suggested_create, or suggested_update. Use view to show an existing automation, create/update/delete to mutate immediately, and suggested_create/suggested_update to present a proposal for the user to review.

kind.description: One of cron or heartbeat. Required for create, update, suggested_create, and suggested_update. Use cron for detached workspace jobs. Use heartbeat when the user wants this thread to wake up later and continue the conversation.

name.description: Short human-readable automation name. If the user does not provide one, choose a concise name.

prompt.description: The automation prompt. Describe only the task itself; do not include schedule, workspace, or thread details because those are provided separately. Keep it self-sufficient, include output expectations when useful, and do not ask it to write a file or announce nothing to do unless the user explicitly asked for that.

rrule.description: RRULE schedule string. Interpret requested times in the user's locale. Cron automations use hourly interval or weekly schedules. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can use minute-based intervals such as FREQ=MINUTELY;INTERVAL=30 or daily/weekly wall-clock schedules.

cwds.description: Cron automations only. Workspace directories for the automation; can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.

destination.description: Optional automation destination. Use thread for heartbeat automations attached to the current local thread.

executionEnvironment.description: One of worktree or local. Cron automations only.

localEnvironmentConfigPath.description: Optional local environment config path for worktree setup scripts. Immediate worktree create calls with a non-null value and immediate worktree update calls that preserve or set a setup config are rejected; use suggested_create/suggested_update for user review. Pass null to clear or run without setup. Cron automations only.

model.description: Model to use for cron automations.

reasoningEffort.description: Reasoning effort to use for cron automations. One of none, minimal, low, medium, high, or xhigh.

targetThreadId.description: Target thread id for heartbeat automations. Prefer destination=thread for the current local thread instead of inventing or copying raw thread ids.

status.description: One of ACTIVE or PAUSED. Default to ACTIVE unless the user asks to start paused.

dynamic_tools.name: read_thread_terminal
dynamic_tools.description: Read the current app terminal output for this desktop thread. Use it when you need shell output or the current prompt before deciding the next step. This tool takes no arguments.

dynamic_tools.name: load_workspace_dependencies
dynamic_tools.description: Locate the configured bundled workspace dependency runtime paths for this local desktop thread, including Node.js, Python, and useful libraries for working with spreadsheets, slide decks, Word documents, and PDFs. This is read-only and takes no arguments.
